### 1. Organizing Instruction and Study to Improve Student Learning

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/1"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [cognitive-science, formative-assessment, k-12, learning-engineering, math-strategies]
```

Published September 2007. Panel: Harold Pashler (chair, UC San Diego), Patrice M. Bain, Brian A. Bottge, Arthur Graesser, Kenneth Koedinger (CMU), Mark McDaniel, Janet Metcalfe. Seven recommendations for using learning and study time effectively in subjects requiring content mastery (science, social studies, mathematics): spacing content review, alternating worked examples with practice problems, combining visual and verbal instruction, linking abstract and concrete concepts, strategic use of quizzes, efficient study time allocation, and deep explanatory questioning.

### 2. Using Student Achievement Data to Support Instructional Decision Making

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/12"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [data-sharing, formative-assessment, k-12, learning-engineering, professional-development]
```

Published September 2009. Panel: Laura Hamilton (chair, RAND Corporation), Richard Halverson, Sharnell S. Jackson, Ellen Mandinach, Jonathan A. Supovitz, Jeffrey C. Wayman. Five recommendations for leveraging student data to monitor academic progress and evaluate teaching: establishing schoolwide data systems, fostering a data-informed culture, integrating data review into instructional improvement cycles, teaching students to interpret their own performance data for goal-setting, and implementing district-level evidence infrastructure.

---

---

### 3. Improving Mathematical Problem Solving in Grades 4 Through 8

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/16"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, math-education, math-strategies, math-word-problems]
```

Published May 2012, revised October 2018. Panel: John Woodward (chair), Sybilla Beckmann, Mark Driscoll, Megan Franke, Patricia Herzig, Asha Jitendra, Kenneth R. Koedinger (CMU), Philip Ogbuehi. Prepared by Mathematica. Five recommendations for teachers, math coaches, and curriculum developers targeting grades 4–8: preparing problems for whole-class instruction, supporting student self-monitoring and reflection, teaching visual representation, exposing students to multiple solution strategies, and helping students recognize mathematical concepts and notation.

---

---

### 4. Teaching Math to Young Children

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/18"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, k-12, learning-engineering, math-education, prekindergarten]
```

Published November 2013. Panel: Douglas Frye (chair), Arthur J. Baroody, Margaret Burchinal, Sharon M. Carver, Nancy C. Jordan, Judy McDowell. Five recommendations for preschool, prekindergarten, and kindergarten educators: teaching number and operations using developmental progression (Moderate Evidence), teaching geometry and patterns developmentally (Minimal Evidence), using progress monitoring aligned to individual child knowledge (Minimal Evidence), teaching mathematical perspectives on everyday situations (Minimal Evidence), and dedicating daily math time integrated throughout instruction (Minimal Evidence). Each recommendation includes implementation steps and solutions for common obstacles.

---

---

### 5. Teaching Academic Content and Literacy to English Learners in Elementary and Middle School

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/19"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [english-learners, k-12, learning-engineering, professional-development]
```

Published April 2014. Panel: Scott Baker (chair), Esther Geva, Michael J. Kieffer, Nonie Lesaux, Sylvia Linan-Thompson, Joan Morris, C. Patrick Proctor, Randi Russell. Research staff: Joseph Dimino, Kelly Haymond, Russell Gersten, Madhavi Jayanthi, Rebecca Newman-Gonchar. Four recommendations for elementary and middle school educators: intensive academic vocabulary instruction using varied activities, integration of oral and written English instruction into content areas, structured opportunities for written language development, and small-group interventions for struggling learners. Includes examples aligned to Common Core standards.

### 6. Teaching Strategies for Improving Algebra Knowledge in Middle and High School Students

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/20"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, math-education, math-strategies]
```

Published April 2015, revised January 2019. Panel: Jon R. Star (chair, Harvard University), Anne Foegen, Matthew R. Larson, William G. McCallum, Jane Porath, Rose Mary Zbiek. Three recommendations for middle and high school algebra educators: analyzing algebraic reasoning, utilizing structural representations, and selecting alternative problem-solving approaches. Each recommendation includes implementation steps and common roadblocks.


Published May 2019. Authors: Sarah Costelloe, Kristen Cummings, Brian Freeman, Allan Porowski, Sandra Jo Wilson, Michael Frye. Five recommendations for higher education instructors, instructional designers, and administrators: use of communication tools, digital resources, self-regulated learning strategies, performance feedback, and simulation technologies for problem-solving. Targets postsecondary settings.

### 8. Assisting Students Struggling with Mathematics: Intervention in the Elementary Grades

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/26"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [intelligent-tutoring, k-12, learning-engineering, math-education, math-strategies]
```

Published March 2021. Panel: Lynn S. Fuchs (chair, Vanderbilt), Nicole Bucka, Ben Clarke, Barbara Dougherty, Nancy C. Jordan, Karen S. Karp, John Woodward. Six recommendations for grades K–6, all with strong evidence ratings: systematic instruction, mathematical language instruction, use of concrete representations, number line application, word problem instruction, and timed activities to build fluency. Covers both general student populations and children with disabilities.

### 9. Preparing Young Children for School


Published August 2022. Panel: Margaret Burchinal (chair, University of Virginia), Karen Bierman, Jorge Gonzalez, Kimberly Nelson, Megan McClelland, Jill Pentimonti, David Purpura, Jason Sachs, Julie Sarama, Elizabeth Schlesinger-Devlin, Julie Washington. Seven recommendations distilling contemporary early childhood and preschool research to help prepare young children to benefit from educational opportunities in school settings.

### 10. CAST Universal Design for Learning Guidelines 3.0

```yaml
url: "https://udlguidelines.cast.org"
type: framework
source: "CAST"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [cognitive-science, inclusive-design, k-12, learning-engineering]
```

Released July 30, 2024. Organization: CAST. An educational framework "based on scientific insights into how humans learn" designed as "a tool that can be applied in any discipline or domain to ensure that all learners are able to access and participate in meaningful, challenging learning opportunities." Version 3.0 specifically addresses "critical barriers rooted in biases and systems of exclusion for learners with and without disabilities." Three design principles: Engagement, Representation, and Action & Expression. Overarching goal: learner agency that is "purposeful & reflective, resourceful & authentic, strategic & action-oriented." Downloadable PDF available in 20+ languages. Audience: educators, curriculum developers, learning and instructional designers, researchers, parents.

---

---

### 11. Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence

```yaml
url: "https://www.unesco.org/en/articles/recommendation-ethics-artificial-intelligence?hub=195885"
type: framework
source: "UNESCO"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [ai-ethics, ai-policy, higher-ed, k-12]
```

Adopted 2021, published 2022. UNESCO's inaugural global standard on AI ethics, addressed to all 194 UNESCO member states. Establishes that "the protection of human rights and dignity is the cornerstone" of the framework. Covers transparency, fairness, and human oversight of AI systems across Policy Action Areas addressing data governance, environmental concerns, gender equity, education, research, health, and social wellbeing.

---

---

Published 2024. Authors: Fengchun Miao, Kelly Shiohira, Natalie Lao. Four core competency areas for students (K-12 and higher education): "a human-centred mindset," "ethics of AI," "AI techniques and applications," and "AI system design." Direct PDF available in English, Arabic, French, Portuguese, Spanish, and Vietnamese at unesdoc.unesco.org (access from direct UNESDOC URL was blocked during audit; description sourced from UNESCO article page).

### 15. EMERALDS: Preparation for Success in Algebra

```yaml
url: "https://achievethecore.org/page/3367/preparation-for-success-in-algebra-exploring-math-education-relationships-by-analyzing-large-data-sets-emeralds"
type: report
source: "Carnegie Mellon University / Educational Testing Service"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [carnegie-learning, cmu-learnlab, k-12, learning-engineering, longitudinal, math-education, math-strategies]
```

Published June 2021. Principal Investigator: David C. Geary (University of Missouri). Institutions: Human-Computer Interaction Institute at Carnegie Mellon University and Educational Testing Service. "One of the largest longitudinal assessments of what mathematics prepares students for success in Algebra" — uses anonymized large-scale data to identify mathematical competencies in earlier grades that form critical foundations for algebraic success, with particular focus on historically underserved student populations. Report hosted at Achieve the Core; full report linked from that page.

---

---

### 16. Lenses on Literature — Evidence for ESSA

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/lenses-on-literature/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [carnegie-learning, computer-assisted-learning, formative-assessment, k-12, learning-engineering]
```

Evidence review published at Evidence for ESSA (Johns Hopkins University). Carnegie Learning's Lenses on Literature (formerly Literacy Design Collaborative) earned a "Promising" (Tier 3) ESSA evidence rating. Instructional framework emphasizing daily, standards-driven reading, writing, and discourse organized around disciplinary literacy with complex texts. Primary study: 18,203 students across Kentucky and Pennsylvania (2012–2013). Effect size: +0.05 average; Kentucky: +0.06 (statistically significant); Pennsylvania: +0.03 (non-significant). Includes scaffolds for multilingual learners, embedded assessments using Stanford-aligned rubrics, and professional development with coaching and data analysis.

---

---

### 18. Breaking With the Past: Embracing Digital Transformation in Education

```yaml
url: "https://digitalpromise.dspacedirect.org/items/6559b261-d6d7-4184-9116-16eb93a9c212"
type: report
source: "Digital Promise"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: "10.51388/20.500.12265/176"
license: null
tags: [digital-learning-platforms, digital-promise, learning-engineering, personalized-learning]
```

Digital Promise report arguing for digital transformation of teaching and learning at scale across U.S. schools. Compares traditional one-size-fits-all instruction to learner-centered, personalized frameworks and examines how digital technologies can enable the transition—drawing on how other industries have used innovation to drive systemic change.

---

### 19. Computational Thinking for an Inclusive World

```yaml
url: "https://digitalpromise.dspacedirect.org/handle/20.500.12265/138"
type: report
source: "Digital Promise"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, digital-promise, inclusive-design, k-12, learning-engineering]
```

Digital Promise report calling on educators to integrate computational thinking across PreK–12 disciplinary learning with an explicit equity focus, addressing persistent gaps in access to computing education for marginalized students. Argues that CT skills are important for all students—not only future computer science students—and issues two calls to action for designing inclusive computing learning opportunities.

### 20. Shifting Mindsets: Designing Lessons for Learner Variability

```yaml
url: "https://digitalpromise.dspacedirect.org/handle/20.500.12265/157"
type: report
source: "Digital Promise"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [digital-promise, learning-engineering, personalized-learning, professional-development]
```

Digital Promise pilot study conducted with DonorsChoose examining whether teachers could use the Learner Variability Navigator (LVN)—a free, open-source web app curating whole-child research and evidence-based strategies—to customize instruction for diverse learners. Found that even brief use of the tool encouraged teachers to apply research-based strategies addressing learner variability across PreK–12 classrooms.

---

---

### 21. Assisting Students Struggling with Mathematics: Response to Intervention (RtI) for Elementary and Middle Schools

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/2"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, math-education, math-strategies, response-to-intervention, wwc]
```

Published April 2009. Panel: Russell Gersten (chair, Instructional Research Group), Sybilla Beckmann, Benjamin Clarke, Anne Foegen, Laurel Marsh, Jon R. Star, Bradley Witzel. Eight recommendations for elementary and middle school math intervention using the Response to Intervention framework: screen all students and provide targeted support (Moderate), emphasize whole numbers K–5 and rational numbers grades 4–8 (Minimal), use explicit and systematic instruction (Strong), teach problem solving via common structural patterns (Strong), incorporate visual representations (Moderate), build fact fluency with approximately 10 minutes per session (Moderate), monitor student progress throughout intervention (Minimal), and include motivational strategies in secondary and tertiary intervention tiers (Minimal).

---

---

### 22. Assisting Students Struggling with Reading: Response to Intervention (RtI) and Multi-Tier Intervention in the Primary Grades

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/3"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, literacy, response-to-intervention, wwc]
```

Published February 2009. Panel: Russell Gersten (chair), Donald Compton, Carol M. Connor, Joseph Dimino, Lana Santoro, Sylvia Linan-Thompson, W. David Tilly. Five recommendations for primary-grade reading intervention using a multi-tier approach: screen all students for reading problems at the beginning and middle of the year (Moderate), provide differentiated reading instruction based on current assessments (Minimal), deliver intensive systematic instruction on up to three foundational reading skills in small groups for below-benchmark students (Strong), monitor tier 2 student progress monthly (Minimal), and deliver daily intensive instruction to tier 3 students showing minimal progress (Minimal).

---

---

### 23. Reducing Behavior Problems in the Elementary School Classroom

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/4"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, sel, wwc]
```

Published September 2008. Panel: Michael Epstein (chair, University of Nebraska, Lincoln), Marc Atkins, Douglas Cullinan, Krista Kutash, Robin Weaver. Five recommendations for elementary classroom behavior management: identify specific conditions triggering problem behavior (Moderate), modify the classroom environment to remove triggers (Strong), actively teach socially and behaviorally appropriate replacement skills (Strong), leverage relationships with colleagues, families, and behavioral experts (Moderate), and implement schoolwide prevention strategies requiring shared responsibility (Moderate).

---

---

### 24. Encouraging Girls in Math and Science

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/5"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, math-education, wwc]
```

Published September 2007. Panel: Diane F. Halpern (chair, Claremont McKenna), Joshua Aronson (NYU), Nona Reimer, Sandra Simpkins (Arizona State), Jon R. Star (Harvard), Kathryn Wentzel (Maryland). Five recommendations for broadening girls' participation in STEM: teach that academic abilities are expandable and improvable (Moderate), provide prescriptive informational feedback about student performance (Moderate), introduce female role models in math and science fields (Minimal), create learning environments that build curiosity and avoid gender stereotypes (Moderate), and integrate spatial skills training into instruction (Minimal).

---

---

### 25. Effective Literacy and English Language Instruction for English Learners in the Elementary Grades

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/6"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [english-learners, k-12, learning-engineering, literacy, wwc]
```

Published December 2007. Panel: Russell Gersten (chair), Scott K. Baker, Penny Collins, Sylvia Linan-Thompson, Robin Scarcella, Timothy Shanahan. Five recommendations for literacy instruction with elementary English learners: screen for reading problems and monitor progress in English (Strong), provide intensive small-group reading interventions for at-risk students (Strong), deliver extensive and varied vocabulary instruction throughout the school day (Strong), make formal academic English development a key instructional goal beginning in primary grades (Minimal), and schedule regular peer-assisted learning for structured academic collaboration approximately 90 minutes weekly (Strong).

---

---

### 26. Turning Around Chronically Low-Performing Schools

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/7"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, wwc]
```

Published May 2008. Panel: Rebecca Herman (chair, American Institutes for Research), Priscilla Dawson, Thomas Dee, Jay Greene, Rebecca Maynard, Sam Redding, Marlene Darwin. Four recommendations for school turnaround leaders: signal the need for dramatic change with leadership communicating urgency to staff and stakeholders; maintain a consistent focus on improving instruction by leveraging data to set goals and reassess learning continuously; make visible improvements early in the turnaround process to motivate staff and reduce resistance; and build a committed staff aligned with improvement objectives.

---

---

### 27. Improving Adolescent Literacy: Effective Classroom and Intervention Practices

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/8"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, literacy, wwc]
```

Published August 2008. Panel: Michael L. Kamil (chair, Stanford), Geoffrey D. Borman, Janice Dole, Cathleen C. Kral, Terry Salinger, Joseph Torgesen. Five recommendations for grades 5–12 literacy: provide explicit vocabulary instruction (Strong), deliver direct and explicit comprehension strategy instruction (Strong), facilitate extended discussion of text meaning and interpretation (Moderate), increase student motivation and engagement with reading (Moderate), and provide intensive individualized reading interventions by trained specialists for struggling readers (Strong).

---

---

### 28. Structuring Out-of-School Time to Improve Academic Achievement

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/10"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, personalized-learning, wwc]
```

Published July 2009. Panel: Megan Beckett (chair, RAND), Geoffrey Borman, Jeffrey Capizzano, Danette Parsley, Steven Ross, Allen Schirm, Jessica Taylor. Five recommendations for administrators and educators designing out-of-school programs: align the OST program academically with the school day (Minimal), maximize student participation and attendance (Minimal), adapt instruction to individual and small group needs (Moderate), provide engaging learning experiences (Minimal), and assess program performance using results to continuously improve quality (Minimal).

---

---

### 29. Helping Students Navigate the Path to College: What High Schools Can Do

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/11"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, higher-ed, k-12, learning-engineering, wwc]
```

Published September 2009. Panel: William G. Tierney (chair, USC), Thomas Bailey (Columbia), Jill Constantine, Neal Finkelstein (WestEd), Nicole Farmer Hurd (National College Advising Corps). Five recommendations for high schools: provide college-preparatory coursework and ensure ninth graders understand college-readiness requirements; use ongoing assessments to monitor readiness and address skill gaps; foster college aspirations through supportive adult and peer networks; facilitate completion of essential college application steps (Moderate); and enhance financial literacy and support the financial aid application process (Moderate).

---

---

### 30. Improving Reading Comprehension in Kindergarten Through 3rd Grade

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/14"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, k-12, learning-engineering, literacy, wwc]
```

Published September 2010. Panel: Timothy Shanahan (chair, University of Chicago), Kim Callison, Christine Carriere, Nell K. Duke (Michigan State), P. David Pearson (UC-Berkeley), Christopher Schatschneider (Florida State/FCRR), Joseph Torgesen (Florida State/FCRR). Five recommendations for K–3 reading comprehension: train students to apply reading comprehension strategies (Strong), instruct students to leverage text structure for comprehension and retention (Moderate), facilitate substantive dialogue about textual meaning (Minimal), choose materials strategically to foster comprehension growth (Minimal), and establish an engaging and motivating context for reading instruction (Moderate).

---

### 31. Developing Effective Fractions Instruction for Kindergarten Through 8th Grade

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/15"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, math-education, math-strategies, wwc]
```

Published September 2010. Panel: Robert Siegler (chair, Carnegie Mellon University), Thomas Carpenter, Francis Fennell, David Geary (University of Missouri), James Lewis, Yukari Okamoto, Laurie Thompson, Jonathan Wray. Five recommendations for fractions instruction grades K–8: build on students' informal understanding of sharing and proportionality; establish that fractions are numbers that expand the number system beyond whole numbers; support understanding of the reasoning behind fraction computation procedures; develop conceptual understanding of ratio, rate, and proportion before introducing cross-multiplication; and improve teachers' own fraction knowledge through professional development. Emphasizes number lines as a central teaching tool across grade levels.

---

---

### 32. Teaching Elementary School Students to Be Effective Writers

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/17"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, literacy, writing-instruction, wwc]
```

Published June 2012, revised October 2018. Panel: Steve Graham (chair, Arizona State), Alisha Bollinger, Carol Booth Olson (UC Irvine), Catherine D'Aoust, Charles MacArthur (University of Delaware), Deborah McCutchen (University of Washington), Natalie Olinghouse (University of Connecticut). Four recommendations for grades 1–6 writing instruction: provide daily time for students to write (Minimal), teach students to use the writing process for a variety of purposes (Strong), teach fluency with handwriting, spelling, sentence construction, typing, and word processing (Moderate), and create an engaged community of writers (Minimal).

---

---

### 33. Foundational Skills to Support Reading for Understanding in Kindergarten Through 3rd Grade

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/21"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, k-12, learning-engineering, literacy, wwc]
```

Published July 2016, revised December 2019. Panel: Barbara Foorman (chair, Florida State/FCRR), Michael Coyne, Carolyn A. Denton, Joseph Dimino, Lynda Hayes, Laura Justice, Warnick Lewis, Richard Wagner (Florida State/FCRR). Four recommendations for K–3 foundational reading: teach academic language skills including inferential language and vocabulary (Minimal), develop phonological awareness and sound-letter links (Strong), teach decoding, word analysis, and written word recognition (Strong), and ensure each student reads connected text daily to build accuracy, fluency, and comprehension (Moderate).

---

---

### 34. Teaching Secondary Students to Write Effectively

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/22"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, literacy, writing-instruction, wwc]
```

Published November 2016, revised December 2019. Panel: Steve Graham (chair, Arizona State), Jill Fitzgerald, Linda D. Friedrich (National Writing Project), Katie Greene, James S. Kim (Harvard), Carol Booth Olson (UC Irvine). Three recommendations for grades 6–12 writing instruction across all subject areas: explicitly teach appropriate writing strategies using a Model-Practice-Reflect instructional cycle (Strong), integrate writing and reading to emphasize key writing features (Moderate), and use assessments of student writing to inform instruction and feedback (Minimal).

---

---

### 35. Strategies for Postsecondary Students in Developmental Education

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/23"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, higher-ed, learning-engineering, personalized-learning, wwc]
```

Published November 2016. Panel: Thomas Bailey (chair, Teachers College, Columbia), Joanne Bashford, Angela Boatman (Vanderbilt), John Squires (Southern Regional Education Board), Michael Weiss (MDRC). Six recommendations for college administrators, advisors, and faculty serving academically underprepared postsecondary students: use multiple measures to assess readiness and place students (Minimal), enhance advising through required or incentivized regular participation (Moderate), implement monetary performance incentives (Moderate), redesign developmental education through compression or mainstreaming (Minimal), teach self-regulated learning strategies (Minimal), and create comprehensive integrated support programs (Moderate).

---

---

### 36. Preventing Dropout in Secondary Schools

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/24"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [dropout-prevention, k-12, learning-engineering, personalized-learning, wwc]
```

Published September 2017. Panel: Russell W. Rumberger (chair, UC Santa Barbara), Howard Addis, Elaine Allensworth (University of Chicago Consortium), Robert Balfanz (Johns Hopkins), Debra Duardo, Mark Dynarski. Updated version of the 2008 dropout prevention guide, incorporating nine additional years of research. Four recommendations for grades 6–12: monitor all students and proactively intervene on early signs of attendance, behavior, or academic problems (Minimal); deliver tailored support to students who have fallen behind and face substantial barriers (Moderate); involve students through educational offerings bridging learning to college and career while building resilience (Strong); and for schools with many at-risk students, establish small personalized learning communities for better tracking and assistance (Moderate).

---

---

### 37. Designing and Delivering Career Pathways at Community Colleges

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/27"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [career-readiness, higher-ed, learning-engineering, wwc]
```

Published March 2021. Panel: Hope Cotner (chair, CORD), Debra Bragg (University of Washington), Grant Goold, Eric Heiser, Darlene G. Miller (National Council for Workforce Education), Michelle Van Noy (Rutgers). Five recommendations for community college administrators, faculty, and staff: structure pathways enabling students to advance education, secure employment, and progress professionally (Moderate); provide contextualized basic skills training accelerating pathway entry and completion (Moderate); offer flexible schedules and instructional models boosting credential completion (Minimal); coordinate comprehensive student services to enhance credit accumulation (Moderate); and develop strategic employer and community partnerships preparing students for labor market success (Moderate).

---

---

### 38. Effective Advising for Postsecondary Students

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/28"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, higher-ed, learning-engineering, professional-development, wwc]
```

Published October 2021. Panel: Melinda Karp (chair, Phase Two Advisory), Brett McFarlane, Donna Linderman (CUNY), Joe O'Shea (Florida State), Lashawn Richburg-Hayes (Insight Policy Research), Sara Ackerson. Four recommendations for postsecondary advising: design comprehensive advising combining academic and non-academic supports (Moderate); build sustained, personalized advisor-student relationships throughout the college experience (Strong); employ mentoring and coaching to strengthen advising and support student achievement (Strong); and integrate positive incentives into advising structures to boost sustained student participation (Strong).

---

---

### 39. Providing Reading Interventions for Students in Grades 4–9

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/PracticeGuide/29"
type: framework
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, literacy, response-to-intervention, wwc]
```

Published March 2022. Panel: Sharon Vaughn (chair, University of Texas at Austin), Michael J. Kieffer (NYU), Margaret McKeown (Pittsburgh), Deborah K. Reed, Michele Sanchez, Kimberly St. Martin (MiMTSS), Jade Wexler (Maryland). Four recommendations for grades 4–9 reading intervention: build students' decoding skills for complex multisyllabic words (Strong), provide purposeful fluency-building activities (Strong), implement interconnected comprehension practices covering vocabulary, question-and-answer activities, text-gist routines, and self-monitoring (Strong), and provide opportunities for students to practice with challenging stretch text (Moderate).

---

### 40. Knowledge is Power Program (KIPP) Charter Schools

```yaml

description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01

license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, wwc]
```

WWC Intervention Report on KIPP, a nationwide network of free open-enrollment college-preparatory schools in under-resourced communities providing approximately 60% more instructional time than traditional public schools. Reviews 4 studies meeting standards out of 6 eligible. Found positive effects on mathematics and English language arts achievement, potentially positive effects on science and social studies achievement, and no discernible effects on student progression for grades 5–12.

WWC Intervention Report on social skills training for preschool children with disabilities — a collection of behavioral practices teaching age-appropriate communication, problem solving, decision making, self-management, and peer relations in early childhood education settings. ### 138. Effective Teacher Professional Development

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/effective-teacher-professional-development"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [learning-engineering, lpi, professional-development]
```

Learning Policy Institute research synthesis by Darling-Hammond, Hyler, and Gardner (2017) identifying seven features of effective teacher professional development across 35 rigorous studies. Finds effective PD is content-focused, incorporates active learning, supports collaboration, models effective practice, provides coaching and expert support, offers feedback and reflection, and is sustained over time. Concludes that PD meeting these criteria demonstrably improves teacher practice and student learning outcomes.

---

---

### 139. Design Principles for Teacher Preparation: Enacting the Science of Learning and Development

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/sold-design-principles-teacher-preparation-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [higher-ed, learning-engineering, lpi, professional-development]
```

Learning Policy Institute report by Darling-Hammond, Hyler, Wojcikiewicz, and Rushing (2025) presenting five evidence-based design principles for teacher preparation programs grounded in the science of learning and development. Principles address: curriculum grounded in developmental understanding of learners; equitable educator mindsets and skills; rich experiential learning with feedback cycles; pedagogical alignment between teacher educators and candidates; and supportive developmental relationships within programs and PreK–12 school partnerships. Draws on neuroscience, developmental psychology, and exemplary program practices.

---

---

### 140. Community Schools Impact on Student Outcomes: Evidence From California

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/ca-community-schools-impact-student-outcomes-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [attendance, k-12, learning-engineering, lpi, sel]
```

Learning Policy Institute study by Swain, Leung-Gagné, Maier, and Rubinstein (2025) evaluating California's Community Schools Partnership Program (CCSPP), a $4.1 billion state initiative. Analyzes 458 schools using matched difference-in-differences methodology across 2018–19 through 2023–24. Finds 30% greater reduction in chronic absenteeism, ~15% decline in suspensions, and academic gains of 0.06 SD in math and 0.05 SD in ELA (~43 and ~36 additional learning days). Benefits were disproportionately large for Black students, English learners, and socioeconomically disadvantaged students.

---

---

### 141. The State of the Teacher Workforce

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/state-of-teacher-workforce-interactive-0"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, lpi, professional-development]
```

Learning Policy Institute interactive report (updated July 2024) providing state-by-state analysis of teacher workforce conditions across all 50 U.S. states. Assigns each state a teaching attractiveness rating (1–5) measuring compensation, working conditions, and retention factors, and a teacher equity rating (1–5) assessing equitable distribution of qualified educators across demographic groups. Draws from national datasets for 2017–18 through 2022–23 academic years.

---

---

### 142. How People Learn: Brain, Mind, Experience, and School (Expanded Edition)

```yaml
url: "https://www.nationalacademies.org/read/9853/chapter/"
type: report
source: "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, nap]
```

National Research Council expanded edition report (2000) edited by Bransford, Brown, and Cocking synthesizing research on learning science across cognitive, developmental, and neuroscientific perspectives. Bridges theoretical findings with classroom practice by examining how expertise develops, how knowledge transfers, and how children acquire understanding. Provides evidence-based guidance for designing effective learning environments across history, mathematics, and science disciplines. Free online reading via National Academies Press.

---

---

### 143. How People Learn II: Learners, Contexts, and Cultures

```yaml
url: "https://www.nationalacademies.org/publications/24783"
type: report
source: "National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [higher-ed, k-12, learning-engineering, nap]
```

National Academies consensus report (2018) chaired by Cora B. Marrett updating the influential 2000 How People Learn volume. Synthesizes findings from cognitive neuroscience, educational research, and learning technologies with emphasis on sociocultural and environmental factors shaping learning across the lifespan. Addresses learning from infancy through adulthood in both formal and informal settings. Free online reading via National Academies Press.

---

---

### 144. CASEL SEL Framework

```yaml

type: framework
source: "Collaborative for Academic, Social, and Emotional Learning (CASEL)"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [casel, inclusive-design, k-12, learning-engineering, sel]
```

CASEL framework (updated 2020) defining the five core social-emotional competencies — self-awareness, self-management, social awareness, relationship skills, and responsible decision-making — and their implementation across four settings: classrooms, schools, families and caregivers, and communities. The 2020 update strengthened the framework's equity orientation, emphasizing SEL's potential in promoting educational equity and excellence and its adaptability to local cultural contexts.

### 148. Funding Gaps 2018

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/resource/funding-gaps-2018/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [edtrust, k-12, learning-engineering]
```

EdTrust report by Morgan and Amerikaner (2018) documenting school funding disparities across U.S. states and districts. Finds districts serving predominantly students of color receive ~$1,800 (13%) less per student in state and local funding than majority-white districts; high-poverty districts receive ~$1,000 (7%) less than low-poverty districts. Gap widens to $2,000 per student (16%) when adjusted for additional costs of supporting low-income students. Documents state-by-state variation and highlights states achieving more equitable funding.

### 149. Teacher Turnover in the United States: Who Moves, Who Leaves, and Why

```yaml
license: null
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, lpi, professional-development]
```

This comprehensive analysis examines why approximately 1 in 7 public school teachers annually leave their schools or exit the profession, using national data from 2020–22. The report finds that turnover rates have increased roughly 27% since the early 1990s, with disparities concentrated in schools serving predominantly low-income students and students of color. Three primary factors significantly influence retention: teacher salaries, effective school leadership, and overall job satisfaction tied to classroom autonomy and collegial support.

### 155. Strengthening the Educator Pipeline Through Service Scholarships: California's Golden State Teacher Grant Program


This report evaluates California's Golden State Teacher Grant Program, which has distributed over $570 million since 2020–21 to help aspiring teachers afford preparation costs in exchange for committing to teach in high-need schools for two years, supporting more than 28,000 candidates and likely contributing to a 23% increase in California-prepared teachers earning credentials. Nearly 75% of survey respondents cited the grant as influential in their decision to pursue teaching, with particularly strong impact among candidates of color and career changers. ### 156. High-Quality Early Childhood Assessment: Learning From States' Use of Kindergarten Entry Assessments


This report examines kindergarten entry assessments (KEAs) across states, noting that as of 2021, 38 states had adopted KEAs—a fivefold increase over a decade—though concerns arose about inappropriate practices like overly scripted assessments or narrow skill-focused measures that exclude essential developmental domains. The report emphasizes that high-quality KEA content aligns with developmentally appropriate kindergarten standards and measures multiple domains including social-emotional, cognitive, language, and physical development in culturally relevant ways. It recommends states select authentic assessments, build systems supporting teacher instruction and family engagement, and use data to strengthen learning systems rather than evaluate individual providers.

### 158. Community Schools Impact on Student Outcomes: Evidence From California


This report evaluates California's $4.1 billion Community Schools Partnership Program, the nation's largest state-level community schools initiative launched in 2021, using a matched difference-in-differences methodology comparing 458 CCSPP schools to similar non-recipient schools from 2018–19 to 2023–24. Researchers found that community school approaches led to a meaningful reduction in chronic absence averaging 30% greater than comparison schools, along with notable improvements in suspension rates and test scores. The most substantial gains benefited historically underserved students including Black students, English learners, and economically disadvantaged youth.

### 164. School Funding Effectiveness: Evidence From California's Local Control Funding Formula

```yaml
url: "https://learningpolicyinstitute.org/product/school-funding-effectiveness-ca-lcff-report"
type: report
source: "Learning Policy Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, lpi]
```

This comprehensive study examines how California's 2013 Local Control Funding Formula—which directed $18 billion in increased state support to high-need districts over eight years—affected student outcomes across the state's 1,000 districts and 10,000 schools. Tracking 6.2 million K-12 students from 1995–96 through 2018–19, the research finds that a $1,000 per-pupil spending increase yielded full grade-level improvements in math and reading after three years, boosted high school graduation rates by 8.2 percentage points, and decreased suspensions and expulsions particularly for Black students. Instructional spending—class size reduction, teacher salary increases, and reduced turnover—accounted for roughly 84% of the variation in funding effectiveness.

### 165. Lost Opportunities: How Disparate School Discipline Continues to Drive Differences in the Opportunity to Learn

```yaml
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, lpi, school-discipline]
```

This report examines how out-of-school suspensions and other exclusionary discipline practices create substantial learning loss, with particularly severe impacts on students of color, secondary students, and those with disabilities. During the 2015–16 school year alone, the nation lost approximately 11.4 million instructional days due to suspensions—the equivalent of 62,596 years of lost learning. The research documents widespread racial disparities in discipline outcomes and significant failures by districts to report school-policing data as required by federal law.

This report examines approximately 330,000 California students classified as English learners for 7 or more years, analyzing their demographics, school contexts, and academic outcomes compared to peers. The research reveals that long-term English learners are disproportionately boys (56%), economically disadvantaged (89%), and have higher special education needs (28%), while attending schools with fewer resources and lower graduation rates. ### 177. Hard Truths: Why Only Race-Conscious Policies Can Fix Racism in Higher Education

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/hard-truths/"

url_confirmed: true

date_added: 2026-05-01
```

This brief addresses misconceptions about affirmative action by demonstrating that Black and Latino students remain underrepresented at selective colleges and public universities across most states. The report argues that addressing racial inequities in higher education requires explicit race-conscious policy interventions, as income-based alternatives have failed to close opportunity gaps for students of color.

### 178. If You Listen, We Will Stay: Why Teachers of Color Leave and How to Disrupt Teacher Turnover

Released jointly by The Education Trust and Teach Plus, this research examines why educators of color leave the teaching profession at higher rates than their peers despite representing less than 20% of the nation's teaching force. Drawing on focus groups with Black and Latino teachers and case studies of schools successfully retaining faculty of color, the report identifies five key barriers teachers face and five actions effective schools employ to support diverse educators.

### 180. Inequities in Advanced Coursework

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/inequities-in-advanced-coursework/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, edtrust, k-12]
```

This report examines barriers preventing Black and Latino students from accessing AP, IB, and dual enrollment programs, finding that while these students succeed when given opportunities, they remain underrepresented due to three compounding factors: schools serving predominantly students of color offering fewer advanced courses, lower overall enrollment in those courses, and racially diverse schools actively denying access. The report provides recommendations for state and district leaders to expand access and improve equity in advanced coursework enrollment.

### 181. 'Segregation Forever?': The Continued Underrepresentation of Black Undergraduates at the Nation's 122 Most Selective Private Colleges and Universities

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/segregation-forever-the-continued-underrepresentation-of-black-undergraduates-at-the-nations-122-most-selective-private-colleges-and-universities/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, edtrust, higher-ed, longitudinal]
```

EdTrust examined Black student enrollment at 122 selective private colleges and universities in 2000 and 2020, finding that despite nearly 74% of these institutions increasing Black enrollment, 71% still received failing grades for Black student access in 2020. The research reveals that gains were minimal and that elite institutions remain largely inaccessible to Black first-time undergraduates relative to state population demographics.

---

### 184. 5 Things for Advocates to Know About Chronic Absenteeism

```yaml
tags: [attendance, edtrust, k-12]
```

This brief documents the rising epidemic of chronic school absence, noting that 16 million students nationally are now chronically absent — missing 10% or more of school days — with disproportionate impact on low-income students, students of color, multilingual learners, and students with disabilities. The report advocates for evidence-based approaches addressing root causes rather than punitive measures, and highlights the importance of prevention, early intervention, and authentic partnerships with families and communities.

### 187. Holding Students Back: An Inequitable and Ineffective Response to Unfinished Learning


This factsheet examines grade retention as a response to pandemic-related learning loss, arguing that while retention may show short-term test score improvements, research demonstrates it produces negative long-term academic, social, and emotional outcomes — particularly for Black, Latino, and Native students. ### 188. How Affordable Are Public Colleges in Your State? (Affordability Gap)

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/affordability-gap/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, edtrust, higher-ed]
```

This report examines the affordability gap that low-income students face at public colleges across all states, finding that after accounting for grants, scholarships, and part-time work, students from low-income backgrounds still face thousands of dollars in uncovered annual costs. In nearly every state the affordability gap at four-year institutions exceeds $3,000, with the national average around $6,500.

### 190. Parent PLUS Loans Are a Double-Edged Sword for Black Borrowers

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/parent-plus-loans-are-a-double-edged-sword-for-black-borrowers/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, edtrust, higher-ed]
```

This brief examines how Black parents increasingly rely on Parent PLUS loans to fund their children's college education, despite these loans carrying higher interest rates and less favorable terms than federal student loans. The report reveals that structural racism, including racial wage and wealth gaps, forces Black families to borrow heavily, and that Black parent borrowers struggle significantly with repayment — often deferring payments and drawing from retirement accounts at higher rates than White borrowers, creating a multigenerational toll of debt.

---

---

### 191. Income-Driven Repayment Plans Fail Black Borrowers

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/income-driven-repayment-plans-fail-black-borrowers/"

url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, edtrust, higher-ed, qualitative-research]
```

Drawing on qualitative data from EdTrust's National Black Student Debt Study, this brief examines how federal income-driven repayment plans are ineffective at reducing debt for Black borrowers. The authors argue that even recent administrative improvements do not address underlying college affordability issues or help future students, and recommend broad debt cancellation plus creation of a new repayment plan with more favorable terms.

---

### 192. For Student Parents, The Biggest Hurdles to a Higher Education Are Costs and Finding Child Care

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/for-student-parents-the-biggest-hurdles-to-a-higher-education-are-costs-and-finding-child-care/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, dropout-prevention, edtrust, higher-ed]
```

This report examines the financial barriers facing student parents, finding that there is no state in which a student parent can work 10 hours per week at minimum wage and afford both tuition and child care at a public college. Student parents face out-of-pocket costs that are 2 to 5 times higher than peers without children, requiring an average of 52 hours of weekly work per week to cover expenses.

### 193. Building a Diverse & Strong Educator Workforce for Students

```yaml

description_inferred: false

doi: null
license: null
tags: [edtrust, k-12, professional-development]
```

This resource highlights a significant disparity in educator demographics: students of color make up more than 50% of the K-12 public school student population while teachers of color represent less than 20% of educators. Students from underserved backgrounds are four times more likely to attend schools with larger percentages of uncertified and inexperienced teachers, contributing to disparities in discipline rates, graduation outcomes, and educational access.

### 194. Access Granted: School Funding Between Schools in Districts

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/access-granted-school-funding-between-schools-in-districts/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [edtrust, k-12]
```

EdTrust analyzed newly available school-by-school spending data required by the Every Student Succeeds Act to examine funding equity within districts. The analysis reveals that in 97% of districts included, state and local spending was flat or regressive for all student groups examined, with very few students of color, low-income students, or English learners attending schools where funding is allocated to meet their additional needs.

---

### 195. Reassessing ESSA Implementation: An Equity Analysis of School Accountability Systems

EdTrust examined how states implement federal school accountability requirements under the Every Student Succeeds Act, finding that states have a mixed record of centering equity in school ratings. The analysis identifies significant gaps in how states identify low-performing schools and support improvement efforts, and reveals that while some states show promising practices, most have failed to connect school identification processes to robust support systems that would meaningfully improve outcomes for historically underserved students.

### 196. Jim Crow Debt: How Black Borrowers Experience Student Loans

```yaml
url: "https://edtrust.org/rti/jim-crow-debt/"
type: report
source: "The Education Trust"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, edtrust, higher-ed, qualitative-research]
```

This report presents findings from the National Black Student Loan Debt Study, based on a survey of nearly 1,300 Black borrowers and interviews with 100 participants. Key findings reveal that Black borrowers view student loans as problematic rather than beneficial, experience income-driven repayment plans as lifetime debt sentences, and overwhelmingly support complete government debt forgiveness as a racial justice solution, with the research connecting these outcomes to systemic racism and labor market stratification.

EdTrust interviewed 32 Black, Latino, and Asian American students at predominantly White institutions to understand how campus racial climate affects their experiences. The research identified key challenges including underrepresentation of students and faculty of color, limited institutional support, unclear reporting procedures for discrimination incidents, and insufficient mental health resources tailored to students of color.

### 201. Evidence for ESSA: Reading Partners

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/reading-partners/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, k-12, literacy]
```

Reading Partners is a one-to-one tutoring program delivered by trained community volunteers who work with struggling readers in grades 2–5 for two 45-minute sessions per week. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one MDRC randomized controlled trial (n=1,166) showing students gained significantly more on SAT-10 comprehension and other outcomes (effect size +0.10) compared to peers, two-thirds of whom received alternative literacy services.

---

---

### 202. Evidence for ESSA: Success for All

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/success-for-all-whole-class/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, k-12, literacy, professional-development]
```

Success for All is a comprehensive K–5 school reform program combining phonics instruction, cooperative learning, small-group tutoring, and frequent regrouping for high-poverty schools. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on six U.S. studies (n=8,849 students across 207 schools) including two RCTs, with an average effect size of +0.29 and particularly robust effects on foundational reading skills and for lower-achieving students.

---

---

### 203. Evidence for ESSA: Reading Recovery

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/reading-recovery/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, k-12, literacy]
```

Reading Recovery targets first-graders in the lowest 20–30% of their cohort, delivering daily 30-minute one-to-one lessons from specially trained teachers covering fluency, decoding, phonemic awareness, and guided reading. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on four qualifying studies (n=7,756 students) with an average effect size of +0.43 on standardized reading assessments including ITBS, Woodcock, and Gates measures.

---

---

### 204. Evidence for ESSA: Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) — Reading

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/peer-assisted-learning-strategies-pals-reading-elementary-whole-class/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12]
```

PALS is a peer-tutoring approach where first-grade student pairs alternate teacher and learner roles, working through a structured sequence of phonemic awareness, phonics, sound blending, and passage reading with error correction. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (n=1,501 students) with an average effect size of +0.23 on Woodcock reading scales.

---

---

### 205. Evidence for ESSA: Enhanced Core Reading Instruction (ECRI)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/enhanced-core-reading-instruction-ecri/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, professional-development, response-to-intervention]
```

ECRI enhances Tier 2 supplemental reading instruction for struggling first graders by providing teacher professional development and small-group sessions (3–5 students) targeting phonemic awareness, decoding, fluency, and irregular word reading. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one study (n=811 students in Oregon and Massachusetts) with an average effect size of +0.24 on Woodcock-Johnson and SAT-10 measures.

---

---

### 206. Evidence for ESSA: Reading Corps

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/reading-corps/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, k-12, literacy]
```

Reading Corps is an AmeriCorps program that places trained literacy tutors in K–3 classrooms to deliver 20-minute daily supplemental instruction using research-based protocols. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=798 students in Minnesota and Wisconsin) with an average effect size of +0.36, with particularly strong gains in Letter Sound Fluency (+0.74) and Nonsense Word Fluency (+0.62) for K–1 students.

---

---

### 207. Evidence for ESSA: Targeted Reading Instruction (TRI)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/targeted-reading-instruction-tri/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, computer-assisted-learning, k-12, literacy]
```

TRI is a one-to-one tutoring intervention that uses webcam technology to train educators to deliver 15-minute daily individualized reading sessions for struggling K–1 readers, covering oral language, decoding, writing, comprehension, vocabulary, and fluency. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two studies (n=471 students) with an average effect size of +0.51 on letter-word identification, word attack, and passage comprehension measures.

---

---

### 208. Evidence for ESSA: Sound Partners — Struggling Readers

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/sound-partners-struggling-readers/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, k-12, literacy]
```

Sound Partners is a one-on-one paraprofessional tutoring intervention for kindergarten and first-grade struggling readers, providing 30 minutes of daily instruction four days per week for 18–20 weeks in phonics, phonemic awareness, sight words, and decodable text reading. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two qualifying studies (n=488 students) with an average effect size of +0.58, with sustained gains confirmed at two-year follow-up assessments.

---

---

### 209. Evidence for ESSA: QuickReads — Struggling Readers

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/quickreads-struggling-readers/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, k-12, literacy]
```

QuickReads is a supplemental tutoring program for struggling readers in grades 2–5 that pairs students for 30-minute daily sessions four days per week over approximately 20 weeks, emphasizing repeated reading, phonics, and comprehension. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (n=483 students) with an average effect size of +0.21 on GORT and Woodcock reading assessments.

---

---

### 210. Evidence for ESSA: Air Reading

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/air-reading/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, computer-assisted-learning, k-12, literacy]
```

Air Reading is a virtual tutoring program for grades 1–6 that delivers explicit, skills-based reading instruction in small groups of up to four students meeting four times weekly for 30 minutes, with consistent tutors using diagnostic assessment to guide individualized instruction. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=740 students in Texas and Louisiana) with a weighted average effect size of +0.20 across NWEA MAP, DIBELS, LEAP, and STAAR assessments.

---

---

### 211. Evidence for ESSA: Building Assets Reducing Risks (BARR) — Reading

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/building-assets-reducing-risks-barr-reading/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, professional-development]
```

BARR is a training and coaching model for K–12 schools built around structured teacher team meetings, data-driven student support, community engagement, and professional development to improve student outcomes. Evidence for ESSA rates the reading component Strong based on three randomized studies (n=9,398 students) with an average effect size of +0.08, with stronger impacts of +0.21 for struggling readers in high school settings.

---

---

### 212. Evidence for ESSA: Intelligent Tutoring for the Structure Strategy (ITSS)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/intelligent-tutoring-for-the-structure-strategy-itss-secondary/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, intelligent-tutoring, k-12, literacy]
```

ITSS is a web-based intelligent tutoring system that teaches nonfiction text comprehension through text structure categorization, delivered in 30–45-minute weekly sessions in language arts classes with paraprofessional support. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one RCT (n=2,489 seventh graders across 108 classrooms in 25 rural/suburban schools) with an effect size of +0.18 on the Gray Silent Reading Test after 6–7 months of use.

---

---

### 213. Evidence for ESSA: Zearn Math

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/zearn-math/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, k-12, math-education, personalized-learning]
```

Zearn Math is a nonprofit adaptive online supplement for K–8 math instruction featuring guided video lessons using a Concrete-to-Pictorial-to-Abstract progression with mastery-based quizzes. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (including one RCT with 10,000+ students in Texas) with an average effect size of +0.09 on NWEA MAP assessments, with strongest results when schools received dedicated implementation support including professional development and coaching.

---

---

### 214. Evidence for ESSA: ASSISTments

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/assistments/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [automated-feedback, computer-assisted-learning, formative-assessment, k-12, math-education]
```

ASSISTments is an online homework tool for grades 7–8 that provides students with math assistance during practice while giving teachers real-time assessment data on student progress. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (a Maine study and a North Carolina replication with ~6,000 students) with a combined mean effect size of +0.13 on mathematics assessments.

---

### 215. Evidence for ESSA: DreamBox Math

```yaml

```

DreamBox Math is an adaptive game-based online platform that delivers personalized, standards-aligned K–8 math instruction as a supplement to classroom teaching. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=13,320 students in California and North Carolina) with an average effect size of +0.10 on the Number Knowledge Test and state assessments, with strongest impacts for K–2 students.

### 216. Evidence for ESSA: ST Math (Spatial Temporal Math)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/st-math-spatial-temporal-math/"

url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, k-12, math-education]
```

ST Math is a supplemental visual problem-solving program for grades 3–6 built on the science of how the brain learns, using animation and game-based tasks to develop mathematical understanding without language barriers. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on four studies (n=346,248 students) including two cluster-randomized trials in high-poverty schools, with an average effect size of +0.07 on state math assessments.

### 217. Evidence for ESSA: Math in Focus

```yaml

description_inferred: false

```

Math in Focus adapts Singapore's "My Pals Are Here! Maths" curriculum for grades 2–5 using a concrete-to-pictorial-to-abstract progression in carefully scaffolded lessons, supported by extensive professional development including summer training and four full-day sessions during the school year. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on four qualifying studies (n=3,444 students) with an average effect size of +0.15 on standardized math measures.

### 218. Evidence for ESSA: IXL Math

```yaml

license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, formative-assessment, k-12, math-education, personalized-learning]
```

IXL Math is a comprehensive personalized teaching and learning platform featuring adaptive skills practice aligned to state standards, video tutorials, diagnostic assessments, and analytics for monitoring progress at district, school, classroom, and individual levels. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one cluster RCT (n=545 elementary students in Michigan) with an effect size of +0.13 on Renaissance Star Math, with particularly strong impacts for Grade 3, Hispanic, special education, and English learner subgroups.

### 219. Evidence for ESSA: Saga Math

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/saga-math/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-01
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, computer-assisted-learning, k-12, math-education]
```

Saga Math is a high-dosage tutoring intervention for grades 9–10 offering traditional (1:2–1:4) or technology-enhanced formats (1:8 alternating direct tutoring with ALEKS computer-assisted learning), delivered three times weekly for 90 minutes total to supplement regular math instruction. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on three studies (n=5,396+ predominantly Black and Latinx students in Chicago) with an average effect size of +0.14, plus increased math GPAs, reduced course failures, and persistent gains one to two years post-intervention.

### 220. Evidence for ESSA: Math Corps

Math Corps is a tutoring intervention for grades 4–8 where full-time AmeriCorps tutors provide approximately 25 students with 90 minutes of weekly intervention support in small sessions targeting foundational whole and rational number skills and algebraic reasoning. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two RCTs (n=1,164 students) with an average effect size of +0.16 on STAR Math and state assessments.


OGAP Math is a professional learning system for grades 3–5 teachers that develops mathematics content knowledge and formative assessment skills using research-based learning progressions, enabling teachers to analyze student work and identify strategies and misconceptions in real time. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one two-year RCT (n=3,488 students across 61 urban schools near Philadelphia) with an effect size of +0.20 on Pennsylvania state math assessments.

### 222. Evidence for ESSA: Graspable Math — From Here to There (FH2T)

```yaml

license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, k-12, math-education, math-strategies]
```

From Here to There (FH2T) is a digital math game for grade 7 in which students strengthen algebraic skills by dragging and transforming expressions using valid mathematical operations, practicing distribution, factoring, inverse operations, and order of operations. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one study (n=1,119 seventh graders across 10 suburban middle schools) with an effect size of +0.14 on an algebraic understanding assessment after nine 30-minute sessions.

My Math Academy is a game-based adaptive learning system for pre-K through 2nd grade featuring 30 games with 130+ activities, adaptive scaffolding, embedded assessment, and narrative-driven characters to build foundational number sense and operations. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two studies (n=1,209 students in Texas and Southern California) with an average effect size of +0.27 on CIRCLE and TEMA-3 math assessments.

### 224. Evidence for ESSA: Whole Number Foundations (Roots)


Whole Number Foundations is a Tier 2 supplemental intervention for kindergarten and first graders delivered by paraprofessionals in small groups of 1–5, with kindergarteners receiving 50 twenty-minute daily lessons and first graders receiving 60 sessions three to five times weekly targeting procedural fluency and conceptual understanding. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on five studies (n=2,140 students across Oregon, Massachusetts, Texas, and Boston) with an average effect size of +0.19 on SESAT and TEMA-3 measures.

Number Rockets is a scripted small-group tutoring intervention for first graders delivered by retired and substitute teachers in groups of 2–3 students for 40 minutes daily over 17 weeks, progressing from concrete to abstract mathematical tasks to build foundational number sense. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on one study (n=994 students below the 38th percentile) with an effect size of +0.34 on TEMA-3 assessments.

### 226. Evidence for ESSA: Fraction Face-Off!

Fraction Face-Off! is a small-group tutoring intervention for at-risk fourth graders that delivers 35-minute daily sports-themed sessions over 12 weeks (36 days) focused on comparing, ordering, placing on number lines, and understanding equivalencies of fractions. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong based on two studies (n=425 students) with an average effect size of +0.51 on NAEP-derived fraction assessments.

### 227. Evidence for ESSA: Building Assets Reducing Risks (BARR) — Math


BARR is a training and coaching model for K–12 schools that structures teacher team meetings around student relationship data and progress, supplemented by community engagement sessions and professional development, to improve academic outcomes. Evidence for ESSA rates the math component Strong based on three RCTs (n=9,386 students) with an average effect size of +0.09 on NWEA MAP and PSAT/NMSQT math assessments, with a +0.33 effect in one high school cohort.

Second Step is a classroom-based SEL curriculum for K–7 that delivers 22–26 weekly lessons per year targeting social-emotional competence and self-regulation, with differentiated content for elementary (K–5) and middle school (6–7) students. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong in academic and emotional well-being categories based on six qualifying studies (n up to 7,213 elementary and 3,705 middle school students) showing effect sizes of +0.10–+0.11 on learning skills, emotional problems, and hyperactivity measures.

### 229. Evidence for ESSA: Facing History and Ourselves


Facing History and Ourselves provides teacher professional development that uses historical case studies addressing racism, prejudice, and antisemitism to promote students' humane and informed citizenship in grades 7–10. Evidence for ESSA rates it Strong in academic and social relationships categories based on three studies including one large RCT across 62 high schools showing effect sizes of +0.14 to +0.23 on historical understanding, tolerance, civic efficacy, civic engagement, and prosocial behaviors.
