### 486. Urban Institute Education Data Portal — API Documentation and Data Sources

```yaml
url: "https://educationdata.urban.org/documentation/"
type: dataset
source: "Urban Institute"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-03
doi: null
license: null
tags: [higher-ed, k-12, longitudinal, open-datasets]
```

The full API documentation for the Urban Institute's Education Data Portal, which harmonizes and provides unified programmatic access to six major federal education datasets: the Common Core of Data (CCD) school directory and enrollment data, the Civil Rights Data Collection, the Small Area Income and Poverty Estimates, IPEDS postsecondary institution data, the College Scorecard, and the National Historical Geographic Information System (NHGIS). The API supports queries by education level (schools, districts, colleges), geography, time frame, and indicator; code interfaces are available for Stata, R, Python, and JavaScript. Data are openly accessible without authentication for most endpoints; the documentation page lists all currently available endpoints and variables.

### 487. Current Population Survey (CPS) Data at NBER

```yaml
url: "https://www.nber.org/research/data/current-population-survey-cps-data-nber"
type: dataset
source: "NBER"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-03
doi: null
license: null
tags: [higher-ed, k-12, longitudinal, open-datasets]
```

The NBER's maintained archive of the U.S. Current Population Survey, the federal government's primary monthly survey of labor force participation and employment, spanning basic monthly data from 1976 to the present and CPS supplements from 1964 onward. The archive includes the Merged Outgoing Rotation Groups (MORG) files for earnings analysis, the Mare-Winship standardized subsets (1964–1992), and thematic supplement extracts covering schooling, fertility, immigration, and income — making it a key source for longitudinal analysis of educational attainment and its labor market returns. All files are freely downloadable from the NBER website with SAS, SPSS, and Stata data definition files and comprehensive methodology documentation.

### 506. Analyzing Strategies in MATHia with BERT

```yaml
url: "https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-98465-5_15"
type: paper
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, carnegie-learning, math-strategies, nlp]
```

Presents ASTRA (AI-based Strategy Analysis), which uses BERT-based representation learning to discover and analyze math problem-solving strategies at scale in intelligent tutoring system data. The researchers pre-trained BERT using masked language modeling on 6th and 7th-grade student action sequences from Carnegie Learning's MATHia platform, then fine-tuned the embeddings to distinguish successful from unsuccessful strategies. Results showed that single-task strategies predominantly led to success while mixed-task approaches were associated with unsuccessful attempts, with the fine-tuned model achieving approximately 80% accuracy.

---

---

### 507. "Can A Language Model Represent Math Strategies?": Learning Math Strategies from Big Data using BERT

```yaml
url: "https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3706468.3706558"
type: paper
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, math-strategies, nlp]
```

Demonstrates that BERT-based language models can learn mathematical learning strategies at scale using student interaction data. The model is pre-trained using masked language modeling to learn strategy embeddings, then fine-tuned to predict whether students will apply correct problem-solving approaches. Testing across 655 schools showed that strategies learned from one school sample could be effectively adapted with minimal examples to accurately forecast student behavior in other schools.

---

---

### 508. De-identifying Student Personally Identifying Information in Discussion Forum Posts with Large Language Models

```yaml
url: "https://www.emerald.com/ils/article/126/5-6/401/1246753/De-identifying-student-personally-identifying"
type: paper
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, llm-application, pii-privacy]
```

Evaluates the effectiveness of three large language models (GPT-4o, Llama 3.3 70B, and Llama 3.1 8B) in redacting personally identifying information from discussion forum posts in massive open online courses. All models achieved high recall rates exceeding 0.9, successfully identifying PII instances overlooked by humans, but precision was considerably lower (0.262 to 0.579) due to over-redaction of non-PII names and locations. The findings suggest LLMs show promise for de-identification tasks, particularly through hybrid human-AI approaches, though context-dependent performance variations indicate careful evaluation is necessary for new applications.

---

---

### 509. De-Identifying Student Personally Identifying Information with GPT-4

```yaml
url: "https://educationaldatamining.org/edm2024/proceedings/2024.EDM-short-papers.57/2024.EDM-short-papers.57.pdf"
type: paper
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, genai, llm-application, pii-privacy]
```

Evaluates GPT-4's performance in de-identifying student data from 9 Massive Open Online Courses, achieving an average recall of 0.958 for identifying personally identifying information requiring redaction. The tool successfully identifies cases missed by humans during the redaction process, indicating GPT-4 can increase efficiency and enhance quality. However, precision is considerably worse (0.526), with over-redaction of names and locations that do not represent PII, showing a need for further improvement.

---

### 510. Mathematics Teachers' Use of Generative AI to Create Active Learning Experiences

```yaml
url: "https://socialinnovationsjournal.com/index.php/sij/article/view/9994"
type: paper
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, genai, math-education]
```

Examines 16 mathematics teachers using generative AI to modify classroom tasks for generative (active) learning and to engage in novel lesson design activities. Teachers valued the creative potential of generative AI for developing such activities but expressed concerns regarding efficiency, usability, accuracy, and broader systemic considerations. The authors present five policy and practice recommendations based on their findings.

### 511. Parsing Coaching Practice: A Systematic Framework for Describing Coaching Discourse

```yaml
url: "https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/epub/10.1177/23328584241263861"
type: paper
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, coaching]
```

Addresses a significant gap in the coaching literature by developing a taxonomy of coaching "moves" that breaks down specific discourse strategies used by professional learning coaches. The research identifies two key problems: limited comparative evidence about coaching effectiveness, and the field's tendency to focus on abstract concepts like trust-building rather than concrete communication techniques. The proposed taxonomy offers practitioners a shared vocabulary for understanding coach discourse patterns, potentially supporting future investigations into how coaching interactions influence teacher development.

### 512. Teaching Teachers to Use Computer Assisted Learning Effectively: Experimental and Quasi-Experimental Evidence

```yaml
url: "https://www.nber.org/papers/w32388"
type: paper
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, computer-assisted-learning, k-12, rct]
```

Examines a program providing weekly coaching to help educators implement computer-assisted learning for customized student progress in elementary and middle school mathematics. Two field experiments showed significant gains of 0.12-0.22 standard deviations in math performance. The research reveals that meaningful improvements occurred when students received at least 35 minutes of weekly practice, with success factors including strong teacher buy-in, clear implementation timing, and consistent monitoring of student progress with targeted intervention for struggling learners.

---

---

### 513. The Promises and Pitfalls of Using Language Models to Measure Instruction Quality in Education

```yaml
url: "https://aclanthology.org/2024.naacl-long.246/"
type: paper
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, llm-application, nlp]
```

Examines how NLP techniques can assess instructional practices across K-12 classrooms and pre-service teacher settings. Pretrained language models performed comparably to human raters when evaluating simpler, discrete teaching behaviors but struggled with more complex practices requiring higher-level inference. Notably, analyzing only teacher speech yielded strong results for measuring student-centered approaches, reducing concerns about obtaining quality student speech transcriptions.

---

---

### 514. Using a Multi-Agent System and Evidence-Centered Design to Integrate Educator Expertise Within Generated Feedback

```yaml
url: "https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7102/15/10/1273"
type: paper
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, automated-feedback, genai]
```

Proposes a framework for using generative AI to facilitate mathematics teacher learning through automated rehearsals where educators practice teaching skills, obtain feedback, and enhance their methods. The approach has three components: leveraging evidence-centered design modeling to create templates capturing mathematics teaching complexity, converting expertise from these templates into GenAI prompts, and employing a multi-agent system that distributes this expertise across agents to maintain sophistication while simulating rehearsals and delivering feedback.

### 516. New Study Finds Every Minute Spent on Khan Academy Can Lead to Learning Gains

```yaml
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
```

Khan Academy researchers conducted a within-subjects study tracking 100,000 students across two school years and found that increased platform usage correlated with improved MAP Growth test scores. The study design compared each student to themselves over time, eliminating confounding factors like motivation. The findings demonstrate that even modest increases in Khan Academy usage translate to measurable learning gains across different grades and demographics.

### 518. University of Toronto Randomized Controlled Trial of 11K Students Demonstrates a Meaningful Positive Effect of Khan Academy on Student Learning

```yaml
url: "https://blog.khanacademy.org/university-of-toronto-randomized-controlled-trial-of-11k-students-demonstrates-a-meaningful-positive-effect-of-khan-academy-on-student-learning/"

source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, khan-academy, rct]
```

A University of Toronto randomized controlled trial involving 11,000 students found that elementary students (grades 3-6) who used Khan Academy for approximately 35 minutes weekly showed 0.12 to 0.17 standard deviations higher math assessment scores. The study demonstrated meaningful learning improvements when teachers prioritized consistent platform usage, though middle school students saw fewer gains due to lower engagement levels.

### 519. Why Khan Academy Will Be Using "Skills to Proficient" to Measure Learning Outcomes

```yaml
url: "https://blog.khanacademy.org/why-khan-academy-will-be-using-skills-to-proficient-to-measure-learning-outcomes/"

description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, formative-assessment, khan-academy]
```

Khan Academy is adopting "skills to proficient" as its primary metric for measuring student learning, as research shows this indicator correlates with standardized math assessments better than time spent alone. The approach aligns with mastery learning principles, emphasizing that students achieve deeper understanding by reaching higher proficiency levels on fewer skills rather than attempting many skills at shallow levels.

### 523. Finding Belonging: The Search for Comprehensive Measurements of Student Belonging in Math

```yaml
url: "https://repository.lsu.edu/srec_reports/18/"
type: report
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, lsu, math-education, student-belonging]
```

Presents Phase One findings on developing an instrument to measure belonging in mathematics. Encompasses a review and assessment of existing belonging instruments, along with insights from a panel discussion regarding how school-aged students perceive belonging. Key deliverables include a comprehensive definition of belonging, an inventory of current instruments, a multidimensional framework accounting for context and perspective, validated items from existing scales, and a definitionally grounded recommended set of belonging items for future piloting, testing, and validation.

### 525. Analyzing Math Learning Strategies at Scale using AI Representations

```yaml
url: "https://aera25-aera.ipostersessions.com/default.aspx?s=19-AD-E5-B0-78-EB-BC-70-51-B2-77-D5-9E-76-70-D0&guestview=true"
```

AERA 2025 poster presenting ASTRA (AI-based Strategy Analysis), which uses representation learning methods to discover patterns in ITS data for analyzing math strategies. ### 528. Linguistic Features Predicting Math Word Problem Readability Among Less-Skilled Readers

```yaml
url: "https://educationaldatamining.org/EDM2025/proceedings/2025.EDM.short-papers.189/index.html"
type: paper
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, math-word-problems, nlp]
```

Investigates which linguistic features predict math word problem readability for struggling readers, using data from 297,072 students in an intelligent tutoring system. A random forest model achieved the best performance (AUC = .75), revealing that traditional readability metrics were not important to the model's performance. Instead, word count emerged as the most predictive feature, followed by custom semantic magnitude and lexical diversity measures, suggesting that specialized readability assessments for math problems differ substantially from conventional approaches.

---

### 536. Educator Perceptions of Working with DreamBox Learning

### 537. English Learner Reclassification Policy Structures and Student Characteristics

```yaml
url: "https://e4.northwestern.edu/2024/06/25/english-learner-reclassification-policy-structures-and-student-characteristics/"
type: report
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, english-learners, northwestern-e4]
```

This research brief examines how English Learner reclassification policies vary across states and their relationship to student demographics. The authors analyze four distinct groups of state policies based on the kind of reclassification requirements and find that states with larger EL populations tend to implement more complex policies, while those experiencing faster EL growth rely more on English language proficiency assessments alone.

### 538. English Learner Reclassification Policy Structures and Student Outcomes

```yaml
url: "https://e4.northwestern.edu/2024/11/05/english-learner-reclassification-policy-structures-and-student-outcomes/"
type: report
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, english-learners, northwestern-e4]
```

This research brief examines how policy complexity in English Learner reclassification varies across four states and affects student outcomes. The study finds that students in states with more complex reclassification policies are less likely to be eligible for reclassification, particularly when non-English language proficiency assessments are included as requirements. The analysis suggests that more stringent policies may create barriers prolonging EL student status.

---

### 539. Exploring the Impact of School Discipline and Attendance on Academic Achievement

```yaml
url: "https://e4.northwestern.edu/2025/05/05/exploring-the-impact-of-school-discipline-and-attendance-on-academic-achievement/"
type: report
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, attendance, northwestern-e4, school-discipline]
```

This research brief examines how disciplinary actions and attendance patterns affect academic growth among over 66,000 elementary students in a large U.S. school district. The study found that attendance rate was a strong and consistent predictor of academic growth in both math and reading, while suspensions negatively impacted math achievement, underscoring the importance of equitable discipline practices and attendance support.

### 542. Investing Early: The Impact of State Prekindergarten Funding on Kindergarten Academic Outcomes

```yaml
license: null

```

This research examines how state-level investment in pre-K programs for four-year-olds correlates with kindergarten math achievement across 31 school districts in nine states. Using data from over 200,000 students, the study found that kindergarten math growth was strongest in states with consistently high levels of PreK4 investment, and that overall district performance history proved to be a stronger predictor of success than pre-K enrollment alone.

PLUS (Personalized Learning Squared) Tutoring combines human tutors, student math software, and AI analytics to deliver targeted tutoring support during the school day. ### 555. Eedi: Misconceptions Map

```yaml
url: "https://learning-engineering-virtual-institute.org/teams/eedi/"
type: platform
source: "LEVI Math"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, learning-engineering, math-education, personalized-learning]
```

Eedi develops an AI-powered "Misconceptions Map" that analyzes student math errors to identify root conceptual gaps and predict future learning struggles. The system uses a knowledge graph to track how math concepts interconnect, recommending personalized learning steps for each student. A randomized controlled trial involving 3,000 students across 20 schools demonstrated that using Eedi led to 2-4 months of additional learning gain in an academic year.

### 563. pyBKT — Python Bayesian Knowledge Tracing

```yaml
url: "https://github.com/CAHLR/pyBKT"
type: code
source: "UC Berkeley CAHLR"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-06
doi: null
license: null
tags: [intelligent-tutoring, learning-engineering]
```

Python library implementing Bayesian Knowledge Tracing and variants for modeling student cognitive mastery from problem-solving sequences. Supports individual student priors, item-specific parameters, and resource-based learning rates with a scikit-learn-style API. Includes optimized C++ backend reducing fitting time from 1.5 hours to 45 seconds for 5,000 students. Published at EDM 2021, 254 stars, MIT license.

### 570. Pre-K Mathematics with DLM Early Childhood Express Math

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/381"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, math-education, prekindergarten, rct, wwc]
```

Pre-K Mathematics with DLM Early Childhood Express Math is a supplemental preschool curriculum using teacher-guided, small-group activities with concrete manipulatives, combined with take-home activities to develop mathematical knowledge in young children. The WWC reviewed 2 studies meeting standards, finding strong positive effects on general mathematics achievement for preschool children in urban settings with racially and ethnically diverse populations. The program showed no discernible effects on oral language, phonological processing, or print knowledge, indicating its primary strength is mathematics readiness.

---

---

### 571. Literacy Express

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/288"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, english-learners, literacy, prekindergarten, rct, wwc]
```

Literacy Express is a comprehensive preschool curriculum for ages 3–5 that delivers instruction through units covering oral language, emergent literacy, basic math, science, general knowledge, and socioemotional development, accompanied by professional development, teaching materials, and classroom management guidance. The WWC reviewed three studies meeting standards and found the program demonstrated positive effects on oral language, phonological processing, and print knowledge, but no discernible effects on cognition or general mathematics achievement. The evidence base draws primarily from implementations with English learner populations in urban settings in Florida and California.

---

---

### 572. Waterford Early Reading Level One

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/541"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, early-childhood, literacy, prekindergarten, rct, wwc]
```

Waterford Early Reading Level One is a computer-based emergent literacy curriculum for preschool children that targets phonological awareness, letter recognition, and print concepts through computer tutorials, reading placement assessments, and supplementary activities. The WWC identified 2 eligible studies but only 1 met design standards, showing potentially positive effects on print knowledge and no discernible effects on oral language. The limited qualifying evidence constrains the strength of conclusions about the program's overall effectiveness.

---

---

### 573. SpellRead

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/481"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, rct, wwc]
```

SpellRead (formerly SpellRead Phonological Auditory Training) is a reading intervention for grade 2 and above that integrates auditory and visual reading processes through systematic, explicit instruction, teaching students to recognize and manipulate English sounds and practice skills using appropriately leveled texts. The WWC reviewed 2 studies meeting standards and found positive effects on alphabetics and potentially positive effects on both fluency and comprehension, with evidence evaluated at grades 5–6 in a predominantly White student sample. The program also serves special education students and English language learners who are significantly below grade level in reading.

---

---

```yaml
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, rct, wwc]
```

Cooperative Integrated Reading and Composition (CIRC) is a comprehensive reading and writing program for grades 2–8 that combines cooperative learning with literacy instruction, featuring story-related activities, direct comprehension instruction, and integrated language arts tasks completed in pairs and teams, with a Spanish version available for grades 2–5. The WWC reviewed 6 eligible studies, of which 2 met standards, and found potentially positive effects on comprehension and general literacy achievement at the Tier 3 evidence level. The adolescent literacy report covers grades 2–6, with a separate review protocol applied for this age range.

---

---

### 575. Lindamood Phoneme Sequencing (LiPS) — Students with Learning Disabilities

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/279"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, rct, wwc]
```

Lindamood Phoneme Sequencing (LiPS), formerly called Auditory Discrimination in Depth, teaches students to decode words by identifying individual sounds using the oral-motor aspects of sounds to identify and order them within words, along with instruction in letter patterns, sight words, and context clues, targeting kindergarten through grade 3 and struggling or dyslexic readers. In the review focused on students with specific learning disabilities, the WWC found potentially positive effects on alphabetics and reading fluency, and potentially negative effects on writing, based on 1 study meeting standards out of 2 eligible studies. The evidence is classified as Tier 3, reflecting a limited but informative evidence base for this population.

---

---

### 576. Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies — English Language Learners (Reading)

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/366"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [english-learners, k-12, literacy, rct, wwc]
```

Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) is a peer-tutoring program for grades K–6 in which students alternate roles as tutor and learner during 30–35 minute sessions, coaching and correcting one another while teachers assign and regularly reassign pairs based on student needs. This intervention report focuses on reading achievement outcomes for English language learners in grades 3–6, finding potentially positive effects based on 1 study meeting standards out of 3 eligible studies reviewed, at the Tier 3 evidence level. The evidence suggests the peer-tutoring model can support reading gains for English learners in elementary settings.

---

---


Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) is a peer-tutoring program for grades K–6 in which students take turns as tutor and tutee during structured sessions, coaching each other through problems while teachers manage pair assignments based on student abilities. This adolescent literacy review addresses reading comprehension outcomes, finding potentially positive effects based on 1 qualifying study out of 5 eligible studies, with evidence classified at Tier 3. The research examined students from predominantly White, suburban and urban backgrounds across grades 1–6 delivered in small-group settings.


Reading Apprenticeship is a professional development initiative that trains teachers to model reading comprehension strategies and help students develop literacy skills alongside social-emotional learning objectives such as growth mindset and self-efficacy, targeting grade 9 students. The WWC found potentially positive effects on comprehension based on 1 study meeting standards out of 3 reviewed, placing the evidence at Tier 3. The limited evidence base suggests the need for additional high-quality research to strengthen confidence in the intervention's impact on adolescent literacy.

### 579. Read Naturally — Students with Specific Learning Disabilities

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/409"

source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, rct, wwc]
```

Read Naturally is a supplemental reading program combining books, audiotapes, and computer software that uses repeated reading, teacher modeling, and progress monitoring to develop reading proficiency, with students working independently at appropriate reading levels in grades 2–6. In this review focused on students with specific learning disabilities, the WWC found no discernible effects on reading fluency but potentially positive effects on writing achievement, based on 2 studies meeting standards out of 3 eligible studies. The mixed findings across outcome domains highlight the importance of examining intervention effects separately by outcome type and population.

### 580. Reading Recovery — Grade 1 Beginning Reading

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/420"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, rct, response-to-intervention, wwc]
```

Reading Recovery provides one-on-one tutoring to grade 1 students with low literacy achievement, delivered by trained teachers in daily 30-minute sessions over 12–20 weeks, incorporating instruction in phonemic awareness, phonics, vocabulary, fluency, and comprehension tailored to individual student needs. The WWC reviewed 79 eligible studies, finding 3 that met standards, and concluded the intervention demonstrated positive effects on general reading achievement and potentially positive effects on alphabetics, reading fluency, and comprehension at the Tier 3 evidence level. Despite the large number of eligible studies, the small number meeting rigorous design standards limits the confidence in the overall evidence base.

---

---

### 581. Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies — Students with Specific Learning Disabilities

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/569"

license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, math-education, rct, wwc]
```

Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) is a peer-tutoring program for grades K–6 in which students alternate as tutor and tutee during 30–35 minute structured sessions, targeting both reading and mathematics outcomes for students with specific learning disabilities. The WWC reviewed 4 studies meeting standards and found potentially positive effects on reading fluency and comprehension, and no discernible effects on mathematics achievement, with reading outcomes rated at Tier 3 evidence. The findings suggest PALS is most effective for literacy skill development within this population, with more limited impact on math.

### 582. The Incredible Years

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/590"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, k-12, rct, sel, wwc]
```

The Incredible Years is a multi-component program for children ages 0–12 providing training for children, parents, and teachers, with child lessons covering feelings recognition, friendship skills, anger management, problem solving, and school behavior to build social and emotional competencies. The WWC focused on grades PK–2 and found potentially positive effects on both external behavior and social outcomes, based on 1 qualifying study out of 3 eligible studies reviewed, at the Tier 3 evidence level. The program's multi-tier design targeting children, parents, and teachers simultaneously makes it notable among early childhood social-emotional interventions.

### 583. Chemistry That Applies

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/595"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, rct, wwc]
```

Chemistry That Applies is a 24-lesson science instructional unit organized into four clusters in which students explore four chemical reactions — burning, rusting, the decomposition of water, and the reaction of baking soda and vinegar — through hands-on laboratory investigations to understand the law of conservation of matter, targeting grades 8–10. The WWC identified 1 eligible study that met design standards and found potentially positive effects on general science achievement, representing a limited but directionally favorable evidence base. The hands-on, inquiry-based approach distinguishes this unit curriculum from standard lecture-based chemistry instruction.

---

---

### 584. GEMS: The Real Reasons for Seasons

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/620"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, rct, wwc]
```

Great Explorations in Math and Science (GEMS): The Real Reasons for Seasons is a middle school science curriculum unit using models, hands-on investigations, peer-to-peer discussions, reflection, and informational readings to teach scientific concepts about the Sun-Earth connection and seasonal phenomena, targeting grades 6–8. The WWC reviewed 1 eligible study that met standards and found potentially negative effects on general science achievement, making this one of the few WWC-reviewed science programs with an adverse evidence rating. The curriculum's hands-on design contrasts with its negative outcome finding, underscoring the importance of rigorous evaluation.

---


Developmental Summer Bridge Programs are designed to reduce the need for developmental education in college by providing students with accelerated developmental instruction between high school and college, combining academic content with college preparation training. The WWC reviewed 10 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on academic achievement, postsecondary enrollment, or credit accumulation across all three outcome domains. ### 587. TAP: The System for Teacher and Student Advancement


TAP (formerly the Teacher Advancement Program) is a comprehensive educator effectiveness system for pre-K through grade 12 that aims to improve student achievement through leadership opportunities, performance-based salary increases, professional development, teacher evaluations, and performance bonuses to attract, retain, develop, and motivate effective teachers. The WWC reviewed 10 eligible studies, with 2 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on general achievement for students after one year of implementation. The evidence tier was not assigned, indicating the research base was insufficient to establish a tier rating despite the large number of studies reviewed.


The New Teacher Center (NTC) Induction Model is a comprehensive program providing one-on-one mentoring and professional development in a supportive school environment to accelerate the effectiveness of beginning teachers and improve student learning outcomes in kindergarten through grade 6. The WWC reviewed 2 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects across all three teacher retention outcome domains — retention at the school, in the profession, and in the school district. The findings suggest the program's mentoring approach does not have a measurable effect on teacher retention by the metrics studied.

Saxon Algebra I is part of the Saxon Math textbook series covering grades K–12, based on incremental development and continual review of mathematical concepts, emphasizing explicit instruction, continuous review of prior concepts, and frequent cumulative assessments to build fluency in algebra for grades 8–9. First Year Experience Courses for students in developmental education are designed to ease the college transition for students requiring remedial coursework, typically introducing campus resources, providing training in time management and study skills, and addressing student development issues — often linked with or taken concurrently with developmental courses. ```

Saxon Math is a core K–12 mathematics curriculum using an incremental approach to instruction and assessment that limits new content per day and reviews concepts cumulatively through ongoing practice rather than discrete chapters, targeting high school students across multiple courses. The WWC found no discernible effects on math achievement for high school students in this review of multiple Saxon courses, based on studies meeting standards. The findings are consistent with the separate Saxon Algebra I review and suggest limited evidence of effectiveness at the secondary level.


Cognitive Tutor Geometry is a mathematics curriculum combining textbooks and interactive software published by Carnegie Learning, targeting grades 9–12 students in secondary geometry. The WWC reviewed 2 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found potentially negative effects on geometry achievement, delivered as whole-class instruction in urban settings. ### 593. Pivotal Response Training

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/668"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, k-12, rct, wwc]
```

Pivotal Response Training (PRT) is an intervention for children ages 2–18 with Autism Spectrum Disorders that focuses on core affected areas including communication and responsiveness to environmental cues, using instruction, child-selected stimuli, attention-focusing, and reward-based encouragement to develop desired behaviors with increasing complexity. The WWC reviewed 40 eligible studies, with 6 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on communication and language competencies for children and students with autism spectrum disorder. The large number of eligible studies reviewed reflects PRT's prevalence in ASD research, though relatively few met rigorous design standards.

### 594. Connected Mathematics Project (CMP)

```yaml
license: null
tags: [k-12, math-education, rct, wwc]
```

Connected Mathematics Project (CMP) uses interactive problems and everyday situations to explore mathematical ideas with a goal of fostering a problem-centered, inquiry-based learning environment, addressing numbers, algebra, geometry/measurement, probability, and statistics across grades 6–8. The WWC reviewed 24 eligible studies, with 2 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on general mathematics achievement, with no evidence that the intervention had an effect on outcomes. The minimal proportion of studies meeting design standards despite the large body of research underscores the difficulty of conducting rigorous evaluations of widely used curricula.

### 595. Saxon Math — Primary Courses (Grades 1–8)


Saxon Math is a core K–12 mathematics curriculum in which new concepts are developed, reviewed, and practiced cumulatively rather than in discrete chapters or units, with limited new content introduced per day and ongoing practice of previously learned material, reviewed here for grades 1–8. The WWC reviewed 26 eligible studies, with 5 meeting standards, and found mixed effects — inconsistent evidence of impact — on mathematics test scores in primary courses. I CAN Learn Algebra is a computer-based math curriculum for secondary students — including middle school, high school, and college — that provides mathematics instruction through a series of interactive lessons that students work on individually at their own computers. Prentice Hall/Pearson Literature (2007–2015) is an English language arts curriculum for grades 6–12 using passages from fiction, nonfiction, poetry, and digital media, with online components for personalized assignments and progress monitoring to build general literacy achievement and comprehension. ### 599. Accelerated Math — Primary Mathematics


Accelerated Math is a software tool by Renaissance Learning for grades K–12 that provides individualized practice problems, scores student work automatically, and generates progress reports to help teachers differentiate mathematics instruction, reviewed here for primary mathematics in grades 2–9. The WWC reviewed 24 eligible studies, with 6 meeting standards, and found mixed effects on mathematics test scores in primary courses, indicating inconsistent evidence of impact. ### 600. NBPTS Certification — Student Achievement

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/689"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, math-education, professional-development, rct, wwc]
```

National Board for Professional Teaching Standards (NBPTS) Certification awards professional certification to educators whose teaching practices meet established standards for accomplished teaching, requiring a computer-based assessment and three portfolio entries completed over 1 to 5 years, evaluated here for effects on student achievement in grades 3–8. The WWC reviewed 39 eligible studies, with 5 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on English language arts achievement and mixed effects on general mathematics achievement. The large number of eligible studies and varied results point to significant heterogeneity in how board certification relates to student outcomes across contexts.


Achieve3000 is a supplemental online literacy program providing differentiated nonfiction reading content to students in grades preK–12, building phonemic awareness, phonics, fluency, comprehension, vocabulary, and writing through instruction tailored to each student's reading ability, reviewed here for beginning readers in grades 2–3. The WWC reviewed 2 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on reading fluency for beginning readers. The no-discernible-effects finding in the beginning reading context suggests the program may be better suited to its adolescent literacy applications reviewed under a separate report.


Passport Reading Journeys is a supplemental curriculum for grades 6–12 combining teacher-led instruction with technology to enhance reading comprehension, vocabulary, word study, and writing skills for struggling readers, organized around themed two-week instructional sequences with whole-class and small-group lessons, independent reading, videos, and computer-based practice. ```

Word Generation is a supplemental program for grades 4–8 that builds students' vocabulary, academic language, and perspective-taking skills through classroom discussion and debate, using interdisciplinary units with daily lessons centered on high-interest topics, with variants including WordGen Weekly and subject-specific editions in science and social studies. ### 604. Literacy Design Collaborative (LDC)

```yaml

url_confirmed: true

date_added: 2026-05-10

license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, professional-development, rct, writing-instruction, wwc]
```

Literacy Design Collaborative (LDC) provides professional development, coaching, and resources to support teachers working collaboratively in their schools to design and implement high-quality, standards-aligned literacy instruction materials focused on reading, research, and writing skills for students in grades 4–8. The WWC reviewed 5 eligible studies, with 3 meeting standards, and found mixed effects on general literacy achievement and no discernible effects on general social studies achievement. The mixed literacy finding across a relatively robust evidence base (3 qualifying studies) suggests LDC may be effective for some implementation contexts but not consistently across all schools.

### 605. Promoting Alternative Thinking Strategies (PATHS)

```yaml
description_inferred: false

license: null
tags: [k-12, rct, sel, wwc]
```

Promoting Alternative Thinking Strategies (PATHS) is an elementary curriculum for grades 1–5 that delivers short lessons focused on self-control, emotional literacy, social competence, positive peer relations, and interpersonal problem-solving skills to enhance social-emotional competencies and reduce aggression and behavior problems. The WWC reviewed 13 eligible studies, with 2 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on academic achievement, social interactions, observed individual behavior, or emotional status for elementary students. The null findings across all four outcome domains are notable given the program's widespread adoption in U.S. schools.


Xtreme Reading is a supplemental literacy curriculum for struggling readers in grades 6–12 designed to improve vocabulary, decoding, fluency, and reading comprehension through teacher-led whole-group instruction, cooperative group work, paired practice, and independent practice along with social skills training and motivational components. The WWC reviewed 3 eligible studies, with 2 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on comprehension and general literacy achievement for struggling readers in grades 6–12. doi: null
```yaml
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, k-12, math-education, rct, wwc]
```

Math Expressions is a mathematics curriculum for pre-K through grade 6 that builds students' conceptual understanding through real-world situations, visual supports including drawings and manipulatives, multiple problem-solving approaches, and opportunities for students to explain their mathematical thinking. The WWC reviewed 3 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on general mathematics achievement for elementary students compared with other mathematics curricula used in the comparison schools. The single qualifying study limits the strength of conclusions that can be drawn about the program's effectiveness.

---

---

### 608. University of Chicago School Mathematics Project (UCSMP) Transitions Math

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/717"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, math-education, rct, wwc]
```

University of Chicago School Mathematics Project (UCSMP) Transitions/Pre-Transition Math is a core mathematics curriculum for grades 6–9 featuring an inquiry-based approach with active learning, hands-on and small-group activities, covering arithmetic, algebra, geometry, probability, and statistics at progressively advanced levels across the two course variants. The WWC reviewed 4 eligible studies, with 3 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on general mathematics achievement or algebra outcomes for students in grades 6–9 compared with other curricula used in study schools. The relatively strong evidence base (3 qualifying studies) with null findings provides a fairly confident negative conclusion about this curriculum's comparative effectiveness.

---

### 609. Social Belonging Intervention

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/720"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, higher-ed, rct, student-belonging, wwc]
```

Social Belonging interventions aim to reduce the impacts of negative stereotypes that burden students from underrepresented groups — including racial and ethnic minorities, women in engineering, and first-generation college students — and affect their persistence in postsecondary education. The WWC reviewed 11 eligible studies, with 7 meeting standards, and found mixed effects on academic achievement, no discernible effects on college enrollment, and mixed effects on progression in college. The relatively large number of qualifying studies (7) makes this one of the better-evidenced postsecondary intervention reports, with the mixed findings reflecting heterogeneous outcomes across student populations and institutional contexts.

---

---

### 610. LASER — Science Education Reform (Grades 3–8)

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/721"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, professional-development, rct, wwc]
```

Leadership and Assistance for Science Education Reform (LASER) builds capacity for implementing inquiry-based science curricula through leadership development institutes, professional development for teachers, instructional materials access, assessment support, and partnerships with scientists and community leaders, targeting grades 3–8. The WWC reviewed 2 eligible studies, with 1 meeting standards, and found no discernible effects on general science achievement compared with standard instruction. The no-discernible-effects finding for this systemic capacity-building approach suggests that regional infrastructure development alone may not translate to measurable student achievement gains.

### 611. Words and Concepts (Pre-K Oral Language Software)

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/550"

url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, early-childhood, literacy, rct, wwc]
```

Words and Concepts is a computer software program for pre-K children designed to build oral language abilities including vocabulary, comprehension, word relationships, and conceptual understanding through six units covering vocabulary, categorization, word identification by function, word association, and conceptual learning, also serving children and adults with language-learning disabilities, developmental disabilities, and other special needs. The WWC reviewed 1 eligible study that met standards and found no discernible effects on oral language outcomes for early childhood populations. The program's broad accessibility design for diverse special needs populations distinguishes it from standard early literacy software.

### 612. MyTeachingPartner Pre-K

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/690"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, early-childhood, formative-assessment, professional-development, rct, wwc]
```

MyTeachingPartner Pre-K is a professional development program incorporating a video library of teacher-student interactions, interactive training, a three-credit college course, and web-mediated coaching focused on improving classroom instruction quality based on the Classroom Assessment Scoring System (CLASS), targeting pre-K teachers and classrooms. As of February 2018, the WWC found no studies meeting their design standards for the Teacher Training, Evaluation, and Compensation review protocol, and therefore cannot draw conclusions about the intervention's effectiveness. The program's video-based coaching approach and CLASS alignment represent a distinctive design rationale among early childhood professional development models.

### 613. System of Least Prompts

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/685"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, rct, wwc]
```

System of Least Prompts (SLP), also known as least-to-most prompting, is an instructional practice for students with intellectual disabilities in which teachers establish a structured hierarchy of prompts starting with the least assistive and increasing support until the student performs the skill correctly. The WWC found no qualifying group design studies, and single-case design studies did not reach the threshold required for an evidence rating in any outcome domain. Despite the absence of group-design evidence, SLP is widely used in special education for skill acquisition across academic and functional tasks.

---

---

### 614. Harlem Children's Zone Promise Academy Charter Schools

```yaml

description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10

license: null
tags: [k-12, rct, wwc]
```

Harlem Children's Zone (HCZ) Promise Academy Charter Schools is a non-profit initiative providing community services and operating two public K–12 charter schools featuring extended school days and years, an academic and arts focus, student progress monitoring, teacher quality initiatives, and community support services including counseling and medical care. As of January 2018, the WWC found no studies meeting their design standards and therefore cannot draw conclusions about the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of this intervention. The absence of qualifying evidence reflects the challenges of applying group design standards to unique charter school models with small catchment populations.

---

### 615. ACT Aspire — Transition to College

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/674"

description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10

license: null

```

ACT Aspire is a longitudinal, systematic assessment system for grades 3 through early high school that monitors students' preparation for high school studies and readiness for college and career across five subject areas, designed to support ongoing instructional adjustment toward college and career readiness goals. As of May 2017, the WWC found no studies meeting their design standards within the Transition to College review protocol and therefore cannot draw research-based conclusions about ACT Aspire's effectiveness in improving college transition outcomes. The program's assessment-based design distinguishes it from instructional interventions, reflecting the WWC's broader interest in evaluating tools used to support educational decision-making.


Prentice Hall Literature (1989–2005) is an English language arts curriculum for grades 6–12 emphasizing reading, writing, listening, viewing, speaking, and language skills through thematic organization, genre-focused units, and supplementary materials for differentiated instruction. As of the WWC review, no studies were found meeting design standards within the Adolescent Literacy review protocol, preventing the WWC from drawing research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness. The curriculum's long publication history and widespread adoption contrast with the complete absence of qualifying rigorous evaluations.

### 617. Reciprocal Teaching — Students with Learning Disabilities

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/626"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, rct, wwc]
```

Reciprocal Teaching is an interactive instructional method for improving reading comprehension in which the teacher and students take turns leading a dialogue regarding segments of the text, teaching four core strategies: generating questions, summarizing, clarifying, and predicting, reviewed here for students with specific learning disabilities. The WWC found no studies meeting their evidence standards for this population within the relevant review protocol and therefore cannot draw conclusions about the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of Reciprocal Teaching for students with learning disabilities. The lack of qualifying evidence is notable given the intervention's prominent theoretical basis and widespread discussion in the reading research literature.

---

University of Chicago School Mathematics Project (UCSMP) Advanced Algebra is a core secondary mathematics curriculum emphasizing problem solving, real-world applications, and the use of technology, featuring a student-centered approach with active learning that incorporates reading and flexible lesson structures. As of May 2016, the WWC found no studies meeting their design standards within the Secondary Mathematics review protocol, and therefore cannot draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness. The UCSMP curriculum family includes multiple courses, each reviewed separately, with none of the individual courses having qualifying evaluative evidence in the secondary mathematics protocol.

### 619. Residential Learning Communities

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/629"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, higher-ed, rct, student-belonging, wwc]
```

Residential Learning Communities (also called living-learning programs) are postsecondary programs that integrate students' academic and residential environments by having students live together in dormitories, take classes together, and participate in structured co-curricular and extracurricular activities designed to strengthen academic engagement and social connection. As of November 2014, the WWC found no studies meeting their design standards for this intervention within the Developmental Education review protocol, and therefore cannot draw research-based conclusions about its effectiveness. The absence of qualifying evidence leaves an important gap given the widespread implementation of residential learning communities across U.S. colleges and universities.

---

### 620. Lexia Core5 Reading

```yaml

url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, early-childhood, k-12, literacy, personalized-learning, rct]
```

Lexia Core5 Reading is a blended learning program for PreK–5 that provides explicit, systematic reading instruction through personalized learning paths adapted to individual student skill gaps, combining online instruction with embedded assessments to help educators customize and track student progress. Across four studies involving over 17,000 students, Core5 students showed an average reading achievement effect size of +0.16 compared to matched comparison groups. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

### 621. SIPPS (Systematic Instruction in Phonological Awareness, Phonics, and Sight Words)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/sipps-systematic-instruction-in-phonological-awareness-phonics-and-sight-words-2/"
type: report
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, rct, response-to-intervention]
```

SIPPS is a structured-literacy reading curriculum using explicit instruction in phonological awareness, phonics, and sight words to support both new and struggling readers across three developmental levels, with flexible grouping based on individual student needs. In a quasi-experimental study of over 500 first and second graders, students receiving SIPPS as a Tier 2 intervention scored significantly higher on DIBELS measures than students who did not (effect size = +0.25). Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

### 622. Expository Reading and Writing Course (ERWC)


The Expository Reading and Writing Course (ERWC) is a 12th-grade college-readiness program designed to prepare high school seniors to enter California State University and community colleges without needing remediation in English, providing curriculum materials and several days of professional development and coaching. Teachers focus instruction on text analysis, critical thinking, discussions, oral language development, and writing across genres. A statewide evaluation found ERWC students achieved modestly higher English placement test scores than peers in traditional classrooms (effect size +0.13), earning a Moderate rating from Evidence for ESSA.

The Superkids Reading Program is a phonics-based beginning reading curriculum for grades K–2 that emphasizes phonemic awareness, phonics, vocabulary, comprehension, writing, and spelling. ```

Amira is an AI-powered reading tutor for grades K–4 that provides personalized instruction, assesses oral reading fluency, and generates diagnostic reports for teachers and parents. Two research studies—including a randomized trial of 178 students (effect size +0.64) and a matched study of over 15,000 kindergarten and first-grade students (effect sizes +0.26 and +0.06)—demonstrated consistent positive impacts on early reading outcomes. Exact Path Growth – Reading is a personalized learning platform that accelerates and measures student reading achievement through individualized learning paths and adaptive diagnostic assessments, studied at the 6th-grade level. tags: [k-12, literacy, rct]

Peer-Assisted Learning Strategies (PALS) – Reading – Elementary, Struggling Readers is a first-grade intervention in which children work in structured pairs, alternating teacher and learner roles to develop phonemic awareness, phonics, sound blending, passage reading, and story retelling with error-correction strategies. One qualifying study of 79 students found statistically significant positive effects on Woodcock assessment scales, with an average effect size of +0.59. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

### 641. My Reading Academy

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/my-reading-academy/"

description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10

license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, early-childhood, k-12, literacy, personalized-learning, rct]
```

My Reading Academy is an adaptive game-based curriculum for Pre-K and kindergarten that teaches foundational reading skills through explicit phonemic awareness and phonics instruction combined with engaging, interactive gameplay. A quasi-experimental study of 1,097 students across four districts found kindergarteners significantly outperformed comparison students on state end-of-year literacy assessments (effect size = +0.17), with stronger gains in Alphabetic Knowledge (effect size = +0.32) and for students with lower baseline literacy skills. Rated Moderate by Evidence for ESSA.

Professional Learning Communities at Work (PLC at Work) is a school improvement approach for grades 3–8 that enhances student math learning through teacher collaboration guided by three core principles: focus on learning, collaborative culture, and results orientation, with recurring cycles of collaborative planning, assessment, and intervention. ### 711. How Rule Induction Data Mining Can and Cannot Be Useful for Education Research

```yaml
url: "https://digitalpromise.dspacedirect.org/items/25a027bb-6b5c-42f1-b058-c16843c06e34"
type: report
source: "Digital Promise"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [digital-promise, english-learners]
```

This paper shares insights and recommendations on how rule-induction data mining can and cannot be useful to education research, based on re-analyzing two regression studies with rule induction approaches. Processes and findings were compared to identify whether, in what ways, and why rule-induction could add value. I found that rule-based approaches can provide unique descriptions of the sample that shows at-a-glance, how key predictors relate to each other and to the outcome. They can also identify relationships between variables that held for some subgroups but not others. It was important to clearly understand the difference between mining rules and mining rulesets, as well as the unique

```yaml
license: null
tags: [digital-promise]
```

This report addresses the systemic shift towards skills-first practices in talent management and its implications for organizations supporting frontline workers and jobseekers. Skills-first approaches (which prioritize demonstrated competencies over traditional credentials like degrees) offer promising pathways to career and economic mobility. However, the implementation of these practices across learning and working environments is often siloed and largely lacks critical input of the workers and jobseekers they aim to serve. This research, informed by a steering committee of experts, identifies key systemic challenges and provides a framework for actionable strategies to address these gaps

### 749. Research and the Promise of Personalized Learning

Compounding this are the several terms for personalized learning approaches that are, at times, used interchangeably, including individualized, differentiated, and adaptive. Yet, as personalized learning as an approach for meeting the varying needs of an increasingly diverse learner population has gained traction in the education community, definitions are emerging with more similarities than diferences.

### 753. The many faces of R: Exploring roles for researchers beyond research

```yaml

url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [digital-promise, k-12]
```

Literature on RPP methodology often seems to assume that the main value that researchers provide to education practitioners is their expertise in collecting, interpreting, and communicating data. However, research-side partners can also take on a number of additional roles in RPPs that are of value to practice-side partners, such as supporting internal communication processes, the procurement of external funding for educational materials, and the vetting of educational technology, among others. In this session, Emi Iwatani and Quinn Burke invite you to explore whether and how researchers can and/or should provide value to districts in an RPP beyond being a data expert.

For the past three years, Digital Promise has embarked on an expansive and human-centered endeavor to empower school districts to cultivate context-relevant solutions to the teacher of color workforce disparities. Through Digital Promise’s Inclusive Innovation approach, teachers of color from various school districts were engaged to develop and implement meaningful solutions to address training, recruiting, and/or retaining teachers of color. This report explores how these co-constructed solutions were implemented, their impact, and the potential to scale these efforts to create sustainable change for teachers of color in the present and future.

---

### 823. How School Administrators can Serve as Productive Partners in Teacher Coaching Programs

```yaml
url: "https://digitalpromise.dspacedirect.org/items/a08ba4d8-72c1-4d8e-902c-c529eed160ac"
type: report
source: "Digital Promise"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, digital-promise, k-12, professional-development]
```

When school administrators support instructional coaching, it is more likely to be effective in improving teacher practice. Building teacher buy-in, promoting awareness of the coach’s non-evaluative role, ensuring coaches have adequate time to provide classroom support, meeting consistently with the coach, and trusting the coach to make decisions are all critical components of a successful coaching program. Based on data from our study of instructional coaching across 163 schools, this guide describes specific actions school-based administrators can take to serve as productive partners in instructional coaching programs.

### 827. Making Waves: Reflections on SEERNet’s Progress and a Vision for a New Generation of Education Research

```yaml
doi: null
license: null
tags: [digital-promise]
```

SEERNet is a network of Digital Learning Platforms, researchers, and practitioners with the vision to leverage DLPs as research infrastructure, enabling researchers to ask and answer important questions about learning in ways that are grounded in realistic, widespread use of learning technologies. A first wave of work established the foundation for the research community. The network looks now towards a second wave of work that clarifies research opportunities and builds capacity for the types of research that can be supported. A third wave seeks to expand the network’s efforts towards broader aspirations for better science, engineering, practitioner engagement, and community.

Key evaluation questions included the curriculum's effect on historical thinking skills, its usability, and its impact on student engagement. ### 918. Houston – 2022 City Studies

```yaml
url: "https://credo.stanford.edu/reports/item/houston-tx-2022/"

url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [english-learners, k-12, longitudinal]
```

This CREDO report examines academic performance in public K-12 schools in Houston, Texas by analyzing one-year learning gains students made between school years during the 2018-19 growth period. The study compares Houston students' progress against state averages across charter, magnet, and district schools and disaggregates findings by school sector, race/ethnicity, poverty status, English Language Learner status, special education status, and gender. Houston charter school students—including Black and Hispanic subgroups—generally outperformed state averages in both reading and mathematics.

### 919. Newark – 2022 City Studies

```yaml
url: "https://credo.stanford.edu/reports/item/newark-nj-2022/"
type: report
source: "CREDO at Stanford"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [english-learners, k-12, longitudinal]
```

This CREDO research report examines academic performance in Newark, New Jersey's public K-12 schools during the 2018-19 school year by measuring one-year learning gains and comparing results against state averages across charter, magnet, and district school sectors. Newark students demonstrated stronger math growth than the state average, and Black and Hispanic students in Newark charter schools showed notably stronger gains in both subjects compared to state peers. Findings are disaggregated by school sector, charter management structure, race/ethnicity, and student population including economically disadvantaged, English Language Learners, and special education students.

### 920. Fort Worth – 2022 City Studies

```yaml

description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10

license: null
tags: [english-learners, k-12, longitudinal]
```

This CREDO report analyzes public K-12 school performance in Fort Worth, Texas during the 2018-19 growth period by measuring one-year learning gains and benchmarking results against state averages. Students overall demonstrated weaker learning gains in reading and similar gains in math compared to the state average, with charter schools outperforming district schools in reading. Findings are disaggregated by race/ethnicity, poverty status, English Language Learner status, special education status, and gender, with English Language Learners in charter schools outperforming both state and district counterparts in reading.

### 922. City Studies: Project Meta-Analysis

```yaml
url_confirmed: true

```

CREDO at Stanford's City Studies Project Meta-Analysis provides periodic reports of academic performance for public K-12 schools across selected cities in the United States, offering a foundation for informed evaluation and constructive discussion among stakeholders invested in public education. ### 923. 2023 Pace of Learning Report

```yaml
url: "https://credo.stanford.edu/reports/item/2023-pace-of-learning-report/"
type: report
source: "CREDO at Stanford"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, meta-analysis, personalized-learning]
```

This CREDO report examines solutions to pandemic-related learning losses in K-12 education, presenting a meta-analysis covering sixteen states and offering empirically grounded estimates for student learning recovery. The analysis argues that conventional interventions focused solely on increased instructional time fail to account for individual student learning rates and their variability, and that addressing the pace of learning itself offers greater potential than simply extending teaching time. National Assessment of Educational Progress results showed regression comparable to pre-NCLB performance levels, underscoring the severity of learning loss across many communities.

### 925. Evaluating the Impact of a Mathematics Mastery Learning Platform on Student Achievement

```yaml
url: "https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-72315-5_32"
type: paper
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, carnegie-learning, computer-assisted-learning, k-12, longitudinal, math-education, personalized-learning]
```

Examines the impact of a mathematics mastery learning platform used by over 500,000 U.S. students in grades 3-8 using multi-year longitudinal panel data. The study improved on prior cross-sectional research by treating each student as their own control to eliminate confounding from time-invariant factors, replicating positive findings on platform usage and test scores. Positive treatment effects did not vary across demographic subgroups, and the authors discuss opportunities for enhanced measurement specificity regarding platform effectiveness. Published in EC-TEL 2024 (Technology Enhanced Learning for Inclusive and Equitable Quality Education); authors include Kodi Weatherholtz, Phillip Grimaldi, Bogdan Yamkovenko, and Kelli Millwood Hill.

---

### 926. GADRRS: Guidance and Agreement for Data Harmony, Responsibility, Retention, and Sharing

```yaml
url: "https://lastinger.ufl.edu/data-learning-technology/gadrrs-data-sharing-agreement/"
type: framework
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, data-sharing, lastinger-center, pii-privacy]
```

GADRRS is a universal data sharing agreement developed by the UF Lastinger Center in partnership with the AIMS Collaboratory, offering standardized terms for educational data exchanges among schools, districts, researchers, and education technology providers. The framework prioritizes student privacy by establishing protocols for data security and responsible usage, and aims to increase transparency, establish consistent safety protocols, minimize administrative burden, and expand availability of anonymized datasets for research. The agreement has been reviewed by University of Florida legal counsel and enables organizations to make deliberate choices about data access authorization while maintaining rigorous privacy standards.

---

---

### 927. Motivation and Engagement in the Mathematics Classroom with Kyron Learning

```yaml
url: "https://blog.kyronlearning.com/motivation-and-engagement-in-the-mathematics-classroom-with-kyron-learning"
type: blog-post
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, english-learners, k-12, llm-application, math-education, math-motivation, qualitative-research]
```

Reports findings from a 2024-2025 school year study evaluating whether AI-powered instruction by Kyron Learning could enhance motivation and engagement among middle school students, with particular focus on English Language Learners. The research involved 22 teachers and nearly 1,000 students across 17 schools, employing a four-cycle rapid iteration design with teacher-created interactive lessons compared against traditional instruction. Quantitatively, student math self-perceptions reached statistical significance favoring Kyron in the final cycle; qualitatively, teachers noted stronger emotional engagement and ELL students benefited from features like pausing, replaying, closed captioning, and vocabulary definitions.

---

### 928. What Kids Have to Say About Math: Results from Focus Groups with Middle Schoolers

```yaml
url: "https://repository.lsu.edu/srec_reports/27/"
type: report
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, k-12, lsu, math-education, math-motivation, qualitative-research]
```

Examines middle school students' perspectives on mathematics education through focus groups with 39 students at two urban Baton Rouge schools conducted in spring 2025. Key findings show students valued their teachers highly as central to motivation, but test-related anxiety presented a significant obstacle, and many students preferred hands-on, teacher-led approaches over digital tools. Students identified perceiving real-world relevance and career connections as the strongest motivator, and advocated for normalizing mistakes, enabling grade recovery, and clarifying learning objectives. Authors: H. Colleen Sinclair, Kyle Wilson, Avery Ellis, and Norma Martinez (Louisiana State University at Baton Rouge), 2026.

---

### 931. Longitudinal Analysis of Gender Disparities in Mathematics Achievement among Elementary Students

```yaml
url: "https://e4.northwestern.edu/2026/02/18/longitudinal-analysis-of-gender-disparities-in-mathematics-achievement-among-elementary-students/"
type: report
source: "AIMS Collaboratory"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [aims, k-12, longitudinal, math-education, northwestern-e4]
```

Investigates how gender differences in mathematics performance develop and change throughout elementary and middle school, analyzing five years of student assessment data across two student cohorts. The research finds that female students often begin elementary school with a modest advantage in mathematics that diminishes as grade levels increase, with gender gaps varying based on district characteristics, student subgroups, and prior achievement rather than following fixed patterns. The author concludes that sustained instructional support and equitable classroom conditions are important for addressing disparities across the elementary through middle school years. Published February 18, 2026 by Kadir Bahar via the Northwestern E4 research brief series.

---

---
