```yaml
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, k-12, meta-analysis, school-discipline]
```

This Campbell Collaboration systematic review examines the relationship between school-based law enforcement (SBLE) presence and school outcomes. Analysis of quantitative studies found that schools with SBLE had higher rates of crime and behavior problems than schools without SBLE, driven primarily by increased exclusionary discipline rather than improvements in actual safety. The review concludes that SBLE appears ineffective for maintaining school safety and may produce harmful consequences for students.

---

---

### 933. The Effects of Small Class Sizes on Students' Academic Achievement, Socioemotional Development and Well-Being in Special Education

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/effects-of-small-class-sizes-in-special-education"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, k-12, meta-analysis, qualitative-research]
```

This Campbell systematic review examined the impact of small class sizes in special education on student outcomes, identifying only seven studies across quantitative, qualitative, and mixed-methods designs. Smaller class sizes are the most preferred option among students with special educational needs, their teachers, and school principals due to opportunities for individualized instruction and increased teacher attention. The authors conclude that the impact of small class sizes in special education is under-researched and call for additional research to inform practitioner guidance.

---

---

### 934. The Effects of Flipped Classrooms to Improve Learning Outcomes in Undergraduate Health Professional Education

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/flipped-classrooms-undergraduate-health-professional-students"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, digital-learning-platforms, higher-ed, meta-analysis, rct]
```

This Campbell systematic review examined 45 studies (11 RCTs) involving 8,426 undergraduate health professional students to assess flipped classroom effectiveness. Findings indicate low certainty of evidence suggesting flipped classrooms, which combine pre-recorded lectures with interactive on-campus sessions, may enhance academic performance and student satisfaction compared to traditional lecture-based instruction. Well-designed studies with larger sample sizes are needed for more rigorous evaluation.

---

---

### 935. The Effects of Inclusion on Academic Achievement, Socioemotional Development and Wellbeing of Children with Special Educational Needs

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/inclusion-children-with-special-educational-needs"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, k-12, meta-analysis]
```

This Campbell Collaboration systematic review and meta-analysis examined 15 non-randomized studies to evaluate whether placing children with special needs in inclusive educational settings versus segregated settings affects their academic achievement and psychosocial adjustment. Key findings indicate that inclusion neither increases nor decreases learning and psychosocial adjustment on average, with effect sizes varying considerably across studies. The authors conclude that placement decisions should be individualized rather than following a one-size-fits-all approach.

---

---

### 936. Evidence-Informed vs Evidence-Based Practice Educational Interventions for Improving Knowledge, Attitudes, Understanding and Behaviour Among Undergraduate Students

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/application-of-evidence-in-practice-health-social-care-undergradates"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, higher-ed, meta-analysis]
```

This Campbell systematic review examined the effectiveness of evidence-informed versus evidence-based practice educational interventions for undergraduate health and social care students. The review screened 46 full-text articles but identified no studies examining the relative effectiveness of the two approaches, making it impossible to determine which better facilitates evidence application in healthcare practice. The search covered publications from 1996 through June 2019 across all languages.

---

---

### 937. Single-Track Year-Round Education for Improving Academic Achievement in US K–12 Schools

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/single-track-year-round-k-to-12-schools-usa"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, k-12, meta-analysis]
```

This Campbell systematic review synthesized 30 studies comparing student performance in single-track year-round education schools versus traditional-calendar schools. Average student achievement was higher in both reading and math at year-round schools, with gains roughly equivalent to one month of learning, similar to documented summer learning loss. Schools with shorter summer breaks of four to six weeks demonstrated larger achievement improvements than those with seven to eight week breaks.

---

---

### 938. Recovery Schools for Improving Behavioral and Academic Outcomes Among Students in Recovery from Substance Use Disorders

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/recovery-schools-students-in-recovery-from-substance-use"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [attendance, campbell-collaboration, higher-ed, k-12, meta-analysis]
```

This Campbell systematic review examined the effects of recovery schools on student behavioral and academic outcomes, including one quasi-experimental study of recovery high schools with 194 participants. Findings suggest recovery high schools may reduce students' school absenteeism, marijuana use, and other drug use, though they showed no clear advantage for grades, truancy, or alcohol use. The authors concluded there is insufficient evidence to determine effectiveness and recommend additional rigorous evaluations prior to widespread implementation.

---

---

### 939. What Are the Effects of Teach For America on Math, English Language Arts, and Science Outcomes of K–12 Students in the USA?

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/teach-for-america-math-language-arts-science-achievement-united-states"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, k-12, literacy, math-education, meta-analysis]
```

This Campbell systematic review examined how Teach For America-prepared educators impact student learning across mathematics, English language arts, and science in U.S. K-12 schools. From 919 initial citations, only four studies met rigorous methodological standards; these found no significant reading improvements from first- or second-year TFA elementary teachers versus comparable non-TFA novices, though a modest positive effect emerged for early elementary reading. The authors conclude that insufficient high-quality evidence exists to draw firm policy conclusions about TFA's effectiveness.

---

---

### 940. E-Learning of Evidence-Based Healthcare to Increase Competencies in Healthcare Professionals

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/e-learning-evidence-based-competencies-healthcare-professionals"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, digital-learning-platforms, higher-ed, meta-analysis, rct]
```

This Campbell systematic review examined the effectiveness of e-learning in improving evidence-based healthcare knowledge and practice among healthcare professionals, including 24 trials with 3,825 participants across various disciplines. Pure e-learning improves knowledge and skills but not attitudes and behavior compared to no learning, while showing no advantage over face-to-face approaches; however, blended learning outperforms no learning across all outcomes and surpasses face-to-face learning in improving attitudes and behavior. The authors recommend future research focus on specific e-learning components and comprehensive intervention reporting.

---

---

### 941. Parental, Community and Familial Support Interventions to Improve Children's Literacy in Developing Countries

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/parental-community-family-support-literacy"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, early-childhood, k-12, literacy, meta-analysis]
```

This Campbell systematic review examined 13 studies assessing interventions involving parents, families, or community members to improve literacy for children aged 3-12 in developing countries. Parent training and child-to-child tutoring are not effective, with eight of nine outcomes showing no significant results; however, educational television appears to improve literacy with frequent viewing at three to five times weekly over several months. The review identifies substantial gaps in empirical evidence for most literacy models implemented by governments and NGOs globally.

---

---

### 942. Early Intensive Behavioral Intervention (EIBI) for Young Children with Autism Spectrum Disorders

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/early-intensive-behavioural-intervention-children-autism"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, early-childhood, meta-analysis, rct]
```

This systematic review examined early intensive behavioral intervention involving 20-40 hours weekly of applied behavior analysis for young children with autism, analyzing one RCT and four clinical control trials totaling 203 participants. Results showed positive effects across adaptive behavior, IQ, expressive and receptive language, socialization, and daily living skills, with effect sizes ranging from g=0.42 to g=0.76. However, the current state of evidence is limited due to reliance on non-randomized studies and was rated as low quality, indicating additional randomized trials are needed.

---

---

### 943. School-Based Interventions to Reduce Dating and Sexual Violence

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/school-based-interventions-dating-and-sexual-violence"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, k-12, meta-analysis, school-discipline]
```

This Campbell systematic review examined 23 studies on school-based programs (grades 6-12) designed to prevent teen dating and sexual violence. While interventions showed moderate improvements in knowledge about dating violence and more accepting attitudes toward conflict resolution, they have little impact on actual behavior change. The review concludes that existing programs require redesign to better support behavioral change and recommends future research emphasize measuring behaviors alongside attitudes and expand beyond North America.

---

---

### 944. Relative Effectiveness of Conditional and Unconditional Cash Transfers for Schooling Outcomes in Developing Countries

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/effectiveness-of-cash-transfers-for-schooling-outcomes"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [attendance, campbell-collaboration, k-12, meta-analysis, rct]
```

This Campbell systematic review evaluated 35 studies examining conditional and unconditional cash transfer programmes' effects on education outcomes in low- and middle-income countries. Both program types increase school enrollment compared to no intervention, though effects on learning outcomes remain limited. The analysis reveals that conditions that are strictly monitored and enforced produce substantially larger enrollment gains, increasing odds by 60% versus under 20% for programmes without conditions.

---

---

### 945. Pre-Graduation Transition Services for Persons with Autism Spectrum Disorders: Effects on Employment Outcomes

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/employment-outcomes-persons-with-autism"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, career-readiness, k-12, meta-analysis]
```

This systematic review examined pre-graduation interventions for secondary school-age individuals (ages 14-22) with autism spectrum disorder designed to facilitate competitive employment. After searching 30 databases covering 1943-2011 and narrowing 5,665 citations to 85 full-text reviews, no studies were identified that met all inclusion criteria. The authors concluded that despite regulations supporting transition programming, rigorous experimental research on employment intervention effectiveness remains scarce and urgently needed.

---

---

### 946. Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) Interventions to Improve the Employability and Employment of Young People in Low- and Middle-Income Countries

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/technical-vocational-education-training-interventions"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, career-readiness, meta-analysis]
```

This Campbell systematic review examined 26 studies across multiple regions to assess whether TVET interventions enhanced employment outcomes for youth aged 15-24 in developing nations. TVET interventions have a small but positive effect on all but one of the employment outcomes measured, though considerable variation existed between studies, with lower-quality research showing inflated effects. The review emphasizes the need for more rigorous future program evaluations and notes that selecting affordable and culturally suitable models may be prudent absent stronger comparative evidence.

---

---

### 947. Interventions in Developing Nations for Improving Primary and Secondary School Enrollments

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/improving-school-enrolment-developing-nations"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [attendance, campbell-collaboration, k-12, meta-analysis, rct]
```

This systematic review examined 73 randomized controlled trials and quasi-experiments published through December 2009 to assess interventions in low- and middle-income countries on student enrollment, attendance, graduation, and progression. Results showed positive effects across all outcomes, with enrollment showing the largest average effect size (d=.18) and infrastructure interventions for new schools demonstrating the strongest results (d=.44). Effects were modest, representing three to nine percent increases in positive outcomes, and policymakers should evaluate whether such gains justify implementation costs.

---

---

### 948. Social Skills Groups for People Aged 6 to 21 with Autism Spectrum Disorders

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/social-skills-groups-people-aged-6-to-21-autism-spectrum-disorders"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, k-12, meta-analysis, rct, sel]
```

This systematic review examined five randomized controlled trials involving 196 participants to evaluate whether social skills groups help individuals aged 6-21 with autism spectrum disorder. Findings indicate some evidence that social skills groups improve overall social competence and friendship quality, though no significant improvements were found in emotion recognition, understanding of idioms, or most quality-of-life outcomes. The authors conclude that while social skills groups show promise for enhancing social competence, additional rigorous research is needed to establish more definitive conclusions.

---

---

### 949. School Feeding for Improving the Physical and Psychosocial Health of Disadvantaged Students

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/school-feeding-disadvantaged-students-improving-health"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [attendance, campbell-collaboration, k-12, meta-analysis, rct]
```

This systematic review evaluated whether school feeding programs enhance health outcomes for economically disadvantaged pupils, analyzing 18 studies examining weight gain, height, school attendance, and academic achievement. Results indicated modest positive effects, particularly in lower-income countries where children gained approximately 0.39-0.71 kg over study periods and showed improved attendance and math performance. The reviewers concluded that school meals may provide small benefits and recommended additional well-designed research with standardized outcome reporting.

---

---

### 950. The Effects of School-Based Social Information Processing Interventions on Aggressive Behavior, Part I: Universal Programs

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/school-based-interventions-aggressive-behaviour-part1"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, k-12, meta-analysis, school-discipline, sel]
```

This systematic review analyzed 73 studies of universal classroom programs aimed at improving children's ability to interpret social situations to reduce aggressive and disruptive behaviour. Findings indicate that brief, intensive interventions of 8-16 weeks prove more effective than extended year-long programs, with social information processing interventions training children through six cognitive stages from encoding cues to behavioral enactment. The research demonstrates that emphasizing thought patterns rather than direct behavior modification strengthens children's general social competencies and reduces aggression across diverse populations.

---

---

### 951. The Effects of School-Based Social Information Processing Interventions on Aggressive Behavior, Part II: Selected/Indicated Pull-Out Programs

```yaml
url: "https://www.campbellcollaboration.org/review/school-based-interventions-aggressive-behaviour-part2"
type: review
source: "Campbell Collaboration"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [campbell-collaboration, k-12, meta-analysis, school-discipline, sel]
```

This Campbell systematic review examined school-based education programmes targeting children's ability to interpret social situations to reduce aggressive behaviour, covering 47 studies of interventions delivered to selected children with behavioural problems or at-risk populations. Students who participated exhibited notably reduced aggressive and disruptive conduct compared to non-participants, with particularly strong effects for pull-out programmes serving at-risk groups outside regular classrooms. This companion review to Part I focuses specifically on targeted interventions rather than universal programs and demonstrates stronger effects for higher-risk populations.

---

---

### 954. Combining Early Grade Assessments to Study Literacy Skills


Published April 2026. Addresses considerable variability in literacy assessments taken in Kindergarten through second grade across schools, between multilingual learners and other students, and within students over time. Researchers examined several popular early grade assessments, developed latent scores of literacy development, and found that each assessment provides predictive information toward third-grade literacy outcomes with considerable overlap, enabling the production of metrics for studying school and district policies around literacy development.

Published March 2026. A field scan capturing experiences of Chicago Public School officials and Community-Based Organization leaders who served on the district's Whole School Safety Steering Committee and co-developed the Whole School Safety Framework between 2020 and 2024. Community leaders viewed the co-design process as healing and restorative, finding that the framework helped ground safety plans in individual communities' perspectives, facilitated power-sharing, and supported the reimagining of safety with impact measured across student and family engagement and outcomes.

### 957. Connection, Trust, and Learning: Student Attendance in the Middle and High School Grades Following the COVID-19 Pandemic

Published January 2026. Examines absenteeism in Chicago among students in grades 6–11, comparing post-pandemic to pre-pandemic years, finding that absenteeism increased significantly following COVID-19 while students' academic achievement remained strongly related to their attendance. Notably, school climate is strongly associated with attendance, and the connection is even stronger today than before the COVID-19 pandemic.

### 958. Illinois High School to Career Series: Precarious Prospects and Promising Pathways


Published December 2025. Tracks Illinois high school seniors from 2008–2012 through post-secondary education and into the workforce, examining demographic characteristics, degree attainment, and career earnings variation by household income level. Finds that while higher education correlates with higher earnings across all backgrounds, students from lower-income families earned less than peers with identical credentials, and that some sub-baccalaureate pathways led to good jobs — particularly benefiting men.

### 965. Predictive Power of Grades and Other Indicators for English Learners

### 966. Variability in the Grades Teachers Give: Teacher Grade Value-Add, Mismatch, and Long-Term Effects

```yaml
url: "https://consortium.uchicago.edu/publications/variability-in-the-grades-teachers-give"
type: report
source: "UChicago Consortium on School Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [formative-assessment, k-12, longitudinal, professional-development]
```

Published June 2025. Examines systematic differences in ninth-grade teachers' effects on students' course grades and their lasting influence on academic outcomes, finding that teacher effects are stable across years and predictive of students' long-term outcomes including eleventh-grade ACT scores, graduating GPA, and high school graduation. The analysis reveals that persistent effects from ninth-grade teachers correlate with long-term academic benefits, though the relationship between grading mismatches and outcomes shows more complex patterns.

---

---

### 967. Disparate Teacher Effects, Comparative Advantage, and Match Quality

```yaml
url: "https://consortium.uchicago.edu/publications/disparate-teacher-effects-comparative-advantage-and-match-quality"
type: report
source: "UChicago Consortium on School Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, professional-development]
```

Published June 2025. Addresses whether teacher impacts vary across student subgroups and how this knowledge could enhance policy effectiveness, developing a new analytical approach introducing "revealed comparative advantage" as a measure capturing how teachers affect outcome gaps between groups. The methodological framework has implications for both policy efficiency and equity in education, showing that a teacher's comparative advantage for one subgroup can yield substantial test score improvements for that group with no effect on others.

---

---

### 968. Using the Freshman On-Track Indicator to Improve High Schools in Chicago

```yaml
url: "https://consortium.uchicago.edu/publications/using-the-freshman-on-track-indicator-to-improve-high-schools-in-chicago"
type: report
source: "UChicago Consortium on School Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [attendance, college-access, dropout-prevention, formative-assessment, k-12, longitudinal]
```

Published April 2025. Explains how Chicago Public Schools improved graduation rates through an iterative research-policy-practice process over fifteen years, centering on the Freshman OnTrack indicator that signals a student's likelihood of graduating and is used for goal-setting and accountability. The system includes real-time data reports, diagnostic tools, research findings, and staff support structures designed to keep ninth-grade students on track for high school completion.

---

---

### 969. How Did Chicago Connected Broadband Access Affect Remote Learning During COVID-19?

```yaml
url: "https://consortium.uchicago.edu/publications/how-did-chicago-connected-broadband-access-eligibility-and-participation-affect-remote-learning-engagement-and-achievement-during-the-COVID-19-pandemic"

```

Published March 2025. Examined the educational effects of Chicago Connected, a broadband internet expansion initiative, analyzing whether eligibility for and participation in the program affected engagement in remote learning during fall 2020 and GPAs in fall 2020 and spring 2021 among grades 5–8 students. Provides evidence on how home internet access shaped student academic outcomes during the COVID-19 pandemic's shift to remote instruction.

### 970. Student Experiences through the COVID-19 Pandemic: Attendance, Online Learning Platform Use, and Annual Survey Reports

```yaml
license: null
tags: [attendance, digital-learning-platforms, k-12, longitudinal, sel, student-belonging]
```

Published February 2025. Examines student experiences in Chicago Public Schools across grades K–12 during the remote/hybrid school year (2020–21) and two subsequent recovery years through 2022–23, addressing attendance patterns, online learning platform engagement, and 5Essentials Survey results measuring school relationships and academic engagement. Documents changes in instructional time, student-teacher relationships, social skills, perseverance, and perceived support systems during the pandemic disruption.

### 971. Early Insights from the Revalidation of the Cultivate Survey

```yaml
url: "https://consortium.uchicago.edu/publications/early-insights-from-the-revalidation-of-the-cultivate-survey"
type: report
source: "UChicago Consortium on School Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [formative-assessment, k-12, qualitative-research, sel, student-belonging]
```

Published January 2025. Examines whether the Cultivate Survey accurately and reliably measures students' experiences of learning conditions in Chicago Public Schools and whether improvements in those conditions predict increases in students' sense of feeling valued. Finds the survey reliably captures learning conditions across all student groups without bias, and that improving students' experiences of their learning conditions from fall to spring predicts substantial and positive changes in how valued students feel.

---

---

### 972. English Learners in Chicago Public Schools: A Spotlight on High School Students

```yaml
url: "https://consortium.uchicago.edu/publications/english-learners-in-chicago-public-schools-a-spotlight-on-high-school-students"
type: report
source: "UChicago Consortium on School Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, dropout-prevention, english-learners, k-12, longitudinal]
```

Published November 2024. Examines high school outcomes, college enrollment, and two-year persistence rates for English Learners in Chicago Public Schools, distinguishing between students who remained English Learners in high school and those who previously held that classification. Finds that formerly classified English Learners demonstrated higher-than-average outcomes in GPA, SAT scores, and college persistence, while long-term English Learners faced greater academic challenges and late-arriving English Learners showed mixed results.

---

---

### 973. The Educational Attainment of Chicago Public Schools Students (2023)

```yaml
url: "https://consortium.uchicago.edu/publications/the-educational-attainment-of-Chicago-Public-Schools-students-2023"
type: report
source: "UChicago Consortium on School Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, dropout-prevention, higher-ed, k-12, longitudinal]
```

Published October 2024. Annual report tracking how CPS students progress on the path to and through high school and college, examining high school graduation, college enrollment, and college completion milestones and tracking how rates have changed across time. Calculates a Post-secondary Attainment Index representing the proportion of current CPS ninth-graders projected to complete any degree or certificate from a two-year or four-year college within ten years.

---

### 974. Measuring Patterns of Immediate and Continued College Enrollment among CPS Graduates

Published October 2024. Examines the To&Through Project's definitions and measurement approaches for college enrollment and persistence metrics for Chicago Public Schools graduates, clarifying how immediate college enrollment is defined and how college persistence rates are calculated. Reports rates for both associate and bachelor's degree institutions and explains methodological updates to ensure accurate tracking of student pathways through post-secondary education.

### 975. Removing Police Officers from Chicago Schools: Trends and Outcomes

```yaml
url: "https://consortium.uchicago.edu/publications/removing-police-officers-from-Chicago-schools"
type: report
source: "UChicago Consortium on School Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, school-discipline, student-belonging]
```

Published June 2024. Examines what happened when Chicago Public Schools began removing School Resource Officers from its high schools during the 2020–21 school year, finding that schools removed SROs without detrimental changes in student disciplinary outcomes or perceptions of school climate. Research was conducted by investigators from the UChicago Consortium, University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, and Lurie Children's Hospital's Center for Childhood Resilience.

### 976. Mitigating the Academic Impacts of Proximity to Homicide: The Role of Schools

```yaml
url: "https://consortium.uchicago.edu/publications/mitigating-the-academic-impacts-of-proximity-to-homicide"
type: report
source: "UChicago Consortium on School Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, school-discipline, sel, student-belonging]
```

Published June 2024. Examines how community violence affects Chicago Public Schools students and whether schools can protect them from these impacts, demonstrating that schools can and do mitigate the negative impacts of adversity that young people experience. Key findings reveal that proximity to homicide negatively affected academic performance, but schools with strong climates featuring engaging instruction and trusting relationships helped counteract these effects.

---

---

### 977. Who Benefits From Attending Effective Schools? Examining Heterogeneity in High School Impacts

```yaml
url: "https://consortium.uchicago.edu/publications/who-benefits-from-attending-effective-schools"
type: report
source: "UChicago Consortium on School Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, dropout-prevention, k-12, longitudinal]
```

Published May 2024. Examines how students with different educational backgrounds benefit from attending effective high schools, finding that all students benefit but the least advantaged students experience the largest improvements in high school graduation, college-going, and school-based arrests. Highlights that less advantaged students gain the most from schools' non-test-score dimensions of quality, though more advantaged students are disproportionately likely to attend the most effective schools.

---

---

### 978. The Four Years Fallacy: Four-Year vs. Six-Year Bachelor's Degree Completion Rates

```yaml
url: "https://consortium.uchicago.edu/publications/the-four-years-fallacy"
type: report
source: "UChicago Consortium on School Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, higher-ed, longitudinal]
```

Published December 2023. Joint study by the UChicago Consortium and the To&Through Project examining important differences between four-year and six-year completion rates for Chicago Public Schools graduates who received bachelor's degrees, investigating completion trends for 2004–14 CPS graduates across different colleges, high schools, student GPAs, ACT scores, and demographic groups. Finds that while only 30% of CPS graduates completed degrees within four years, this rate increased to 51% when measured over six years — with significant implications for how institutions and policymakers measure college success.

---

---

### 982. Accelerated Reader

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/12"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, wwc]
```

Accelerated Reader is a computerized supplementary reading tool for grades 1–8 in which students select books based on interest and reading level and then take computerized comprehension quizzes. The WWC reviewed 2 studies that met standards out of 11 eligible studies. The program was found to have mixed effects on comprehension and no discernible effects on reading fluency for beginning readers.

---

### 984. AEGIS (Acquiring Ethical Guidelines for Individual Self Governance)


AEGIS is a K–6 character education program that teaches ethical behavior through seven core concepts—including worth and potential, fairness and justice, and personal integrity—delivered via 35 integrated curriculum lessons using a five-step instructional process. As of June 2007, the WWC found no studies meeting its design standards and was unable to draw research-based conclusions about the program's effectiveness or ineffectiveness.

### 990. ALAS (Achievement for Latinos through Academic Success)

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/22"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [dropout-prevention, english-learners, k-12, wwc]
```

ALAS (Achievement for Latinos through Academic Success) is a dropout prevention program for grades 7–9 that assigns each student a counselor/mentor who monitors attendance, behavior, and grades while providing student training in problem-solving and parent training in school engagement. One qualifying study was reviewed, showing potentially positive effects on both staying in school and progressing in school through ninth grade. The sample was 96% Hispanic, 91% receiving free/reduced-price lunch, and 23% English learners in an urban California setting.

### 999. Arthur (English Language Development)

### 1002. Concept-Oriented Reading Instruction (CORI) — Adolescent Literacy

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/103"
type: report
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, wwc]
```

Concept-Oriented Reading Instruction (CORI) is a grades 3–9 program that integrates reading and science instruction through activities and science books, targeting reading comprehension and reading engagement. This WWC report covers CORI under the Adolescent Literacy review protocol; as of August 2010, no qualifying studies were found that met WWC design standards. A separate WWC review of CORI under the Beginning Reading protocol also found no qualifying studies.

Rural schools frequently struggle with restricted availability of academic and mental health resources. By establishing a cross-district community schools consortium, participating districts were able to "pool and coordinate otherwise out-of-reach resources, strengthening their capacity to expand student access to academic and social supports." ### 1050. The Impact of NBA Math Hoops on Students' Math Achievement: A Randomized Controlled Trial

```yaml
url: "https://www.wested.org/resource/the-impact-of-nba-math-hoops-on-students-math-achievement/"
type: report
source: "WestEd"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [digital-learning-platforms, k-12, math-education, math-motivation, rct]
```

This study evaluated how NBA Math Hoops affects student math performance through a rigorous experimental design. The research found that "students in NBA Math Hoops classrooms scored an average of 0.19 standard deviations higher than peers in STEM enrichment classrooms" — roughly equivalent to advancing from the 50th to 58th percentile. The program demonstrated practical benefits for educators, requiring minimal training and costing under $20 per student while remaining straightforward to implement in out-of-school settings. Published 2026.

### 1052. Friction by Design: A Framework for Centering Learning in the Age of AI

```yaml
url: "https://www.wested.org/resource/friction-by-design/"
type: framework
source: "WestEd"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [ai-ethics, digital-learning-platforms, genai, k-12, personalized-learning]
```

This framework helps educators integrate AI intentionally while preserving meaningful learning. It distinguishes between productive friction — the effort and struggle that enables growth — and unproductive friction that obstructs progress. The guide employs five design lenses (Cognitive Ownership, Productive Struggle, Social Sense-Making, Activation Energy, and Access Barriers) to show how AI can reduce barriers while maintaining the challenge essential to learning. ### 1061. Academically Diverse Classrooms, Deeper Needs: What Teachers Face After the Pandemic

### 1062. Should Kindergartners Be Redshirted? Costs Likely Outweigh Academic Benefits

```yaml
url: "https://www.nwea.org/research/publication/should-kindergartners-be-redshirted-costs-likely-outweigh-academic-benefits/"
type: report
source: "NWEA Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, k-12, longitudinal, prekindergarten]
```

The research examines data on delaying kindergarten entry — known as redshirting. Using assessment results from over three million kindergartners, the study analyzed trends between 2017–2025 and followed one cohort through third grade to determine whether delayed entry provided academic advantages. The findings indicate that the academic costs of delayed entry likely outweigh any benefits, providing quantitative evidence to inform family and district decisions about kindergarten enrollment timing. Published March 2026, by Kuhfeld et al. (NWEA).

### 1065. The Middle School Science Course Landscape: Course Offerings, Enrollment Patterns, and Implications for Assessment

```yaml
url: "https://www.nwea.org/research/publication/the-middle-school-science-course-landscape-course-offerings-enrollment-patterns-and-implications-for-assessment/"
type: report
source: "NWEA Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [formative-assessment, k-12, longitudinal]
```

This research examines middle school science curriculum structures across 2,200 schools. The study finds that "multi-disciplinary course models now dominate versus single-course offerings," while noting disparities in access tied to student demographics. Notably, the research indicates that "students in different course models performed similarly on assessments," suggesting structural course design may not significantly impact achievement outcomes, with implications for science assessment design and equitable course access. Published April 2026, by NWEA researchers.

### 1066. Evaluation of the Networks for School Improvement Initiative: Impacts on Students and Implementation Factors

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/evaluation-of-the-networks-for-school-improvement-initiative-student-outcomes-final-report"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, k-12, longitudinal, professional-development, rct]
```

This evaluation examined how network-based continuous improvement initiatives affected student outcomes through schools' fourth year of participation. The Gates Foundation funded 34 Networks for School Improvement across the country from 2018 to 2025, investing over $300 million in intermediary organizations. The study compared results for students in participating schools against similar students in non-participating schools, finding mixed results — some grade-level interventions improved math scores and course outcomes, while others showed limited impact on graduation rates and college enrollment. Published April 22, 2026, by Mathematica.

### 1067. Trusting Your LLM: Evaluating Statistical Analysis Performance

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/trusting-your-llm"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [ai-policy, genai, learning-engineering, llm-application]
```

A framework evaluating how large language models perform statistical analysis on complex survey data. The research tested 35 questions based on American Community Survey data, comparing reasoning-only versus code-execution workflows. Findings show that "data access and tool invocation substantially improve accuracy, particularly for descriptive tasks, while causal inference performance varies meaningfully across models." The results have direct implications for researchers and policy analysts considering LLM use in education data analysis. Published March 6, 2026, by Mathematica.

### 1068. Early Childhood Educator Pay Equity Fund: Do Economic Returns Change Over Time?

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/early-childhood-educator-pef-do-economic-returns-change-over-time"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, longitudinal, professional-development]
```

This research brief analyzes the economic returns of Washington, DC's Pay Equity Fund in fiscal year 2024. The study found that the program's benefits exceeded costs by $16 million, yielding a 21 percent social return on investment. The analysis examines how the program's shift to a facility payroll model affected its economic efficiency while delivering wage payments to over 4,000 educators and subsidized health insurance benefits, offering a model for other jurisdictions seeking to raise early childhood educator compensation. Published April 21, 2026, by Mathematica.

---

---

### 1069. Jobs in the Balance: The Three-Year Labor Market Impacts of Washington, DC's Early Childhood Educator Pay Equity Fund

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/three-year-labor-market-impacts-of-washington-dcs-early-childhood-educator-pef"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, longitudinal, professional-development, rct]
```

This working paper examines how DC's Pay Equity Fund — the nation's first dedicated public funding initiative to narrow compensation gaps between early childhood educators and public school teachers — affected the labor market over three years. Using federal labor market data and synthetic control methods, researchers found the program increased the educator workforce by approximately 341 workers (11% growth) and raised wages following the transition to a facility payment model, though it did not significantly increase the number of child care establishments. Published April 17, 2026, by Mathematica.

---

---

### 1070. The Impact of Registered and Unregistered Apprenticeship

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/the-impact-of-registered-and-unregistered-apprenticeship"

url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [career-readiness, higher-ed, longitudinal, rct]
```

This research examines employment and earnings outcomes from two federally funded apprenticeship grant programs. The study found that "registered and unregistered apprenticeships had large and significant impacts on employment and earnings in the ninth quarter post enrollment." The research covers IT, healthcare, and advanced manufacturing sectors, providing rigorous causal evidence on the effectiveness of employer-based training models that combine classroom instruction with on-the-job mentorship for workforce development. Published April 1, 2026, by Mathematica.

### 1071. I CAN Learn Geometry

```yaml
url: "https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/wwc/InterventionReport/678"
type: review
source: "What Works Clearinghouse"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, k-12, math-education, wwc]
```

I CAN Learn Geometry is a computer-based math curriculum for students in middle school, high school, and college that delivers math instruction through interactive lessons students complete individually at their own pace. Students must demonstrate mastery of each concept before advancing, and teachers can provide supplemental instruction based on student performance data from the software. As of August 2017, the WWC found no studies meeting its design standards for this intervention, so it could not determine effectiveness or ineffectiveness for improving secondary mathematics outcomes.

---

### 1072. Really Great Reading's Foundational Literacy Programs

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/really-great-reading/"
type: review
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, rct]
```

RGR provides teachers with professional development and structured curriculum resources for explicit instruction in phonological awareness, phonics, word recognition, decoding, and fluency. The program includes digital lesson delivery tools designed to standardize instruction and support diverse learners in grades 1–3. A study of over 1,000 students across 13 urban charter schools found positive results: "Grade 1 and grade 2 students receiving RGR instruction scored higher on the NWEA MAP Reading Growth assessment in winter 2022 (effect size = +0.30, +0.24)," earning a Promising ESSA evidence tier.

---

---

### 1073. mCLASS Intervention (formerly BURST Reading)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/burst-reading/"
type: review
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [formative-assessment, k-12, literacy, rct, response-to-intervention]
```

This reading intervention delivers 10-lesson units targeting specific skills identified through formative assessments conducted three times yearly. Small groups of 3–5 students in grades K–3 receive instruction from teachers or paraprofessionals using "a direct instruction approach, followed by progress monitoring and assessment." One study across 57 schools evaluated the program with 4,022 students, showing positive effects on DIBELS Next and STAR Early Literacy measures (effect size = +0.10), earning a Promising ESSA evidence tier.

---

---

### 1074. Reading Apprenticeship Academic Literacy (RAAL)

```yaml
url: "https://evidenceforessa.org/program/reading-apprenticeship-academic-literacy-raal/"
type: review
source: "Evidence for ESSA"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [coaching, k-12, literacy, professional-development, rct]
```

Reading Apprenticeship Academic Literacy (RAAL) is a high school literacy program (grades 9–12) representing an adaptation of Reading Apprenticeship designed for diverse student populations. Implementation requires a dedicated year-long course taught by at least one teacher, with WestEd providing comprehensive professional development including a 5-day pre-course institute and ongoing coaching. One study examining RAAL against Xtreme Reading and control conditions found participants demonstrated significantly improved state assessment performance, with an average effect size of +0.10 across 2,255 students, earning a Strong ESSA evidence tier.

---

### 1076. Rethinking Summer Slide: The More You Gain, the More You Lose

```yaml
url: "https://www.nwea.org/research/publication/rethinking-summer-slide-the-more-you-gain-the-more-you-lose/"
type: report
source: "NWEA Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, longitudinal]
```

This research draws on data from 3.4 million students assessed with NWEA MAP Growth to examine summer learning loss patterns. The study reveals that students experiencing the greatest academic decline during summer were those who had made the most substantial gains during the preceding school year. The findings challenge conventional explanations for summer learning loss such as program access and school year length.

### 1086. Disparities and Discrimination in Student Discipline by Race and Family Income

```yaml

doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, school-discipline]
```

This study examines racial and socioeconomic disparities in school discipline by comparing punishments given to Black and White students and poor versus nonpoor students involved in the same incidents. The researchers find that Black and poor students are punished more harshly than the students with whom they fight, revealing evidence of discrimination in discipline practices. The research addresses a critical methodological challenge that had previously prevented scholars from identifying discriminatory patterns in student discipline outcomes.

### 1095. Does Four Equal Five? Implementation and Outcomes of the Four-Day School Week

```yaml

description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, longitudinal]
```

This large-scale study examined the implementation and outcomes of four-day school weeks compared to traditional five-day schedules across Idaho, New Mexico, and Oklahoma. Cost savings related to the four-day model were relatively small, and students on the four-day schedule had lower scores over time when compared with peers on a five-day schedule. Results overall were mixed, with stakeholders reporting high satisfaction despite modest academic performance differences.

```yaml
tags: [formative-assessment, k-12, longitudinal]
```

This study analyzed data from 6.7 million U.S. public school students in grades 3–8 to examine academic progress in the 2022–23 school year, finding that progress toward academic recovery stalled. Students require approximately 4.1 additional months of instruction in reading and 4.5 months in mathematics to catch up to pre-pandemic performance levels, with persistent achievement gaps remaining.

---

### 1099. Progress Towards Pandemic Recovery: Continued Signs of Rebounding Achievement at the Start of the 2022-23 School Year

```yaml
url: "https://www.nwea.org/research/publication/progress-towards-pandemic-recovery-continued-signs-of-rebounding-achievement-at-the-start-of-the-2022-23-school-year/"

license: null

```

This research analyzed data from 7 million students in grades 3–8 across 25,000 schools who completed MAP Growth assessments between 2020–21 and fall 2022. The study found that students are rebounding from the impacts of the pandemic with achievement gaps narrowing from their widest point in spring 2021, though student achievement remains lower than in a typical year and full recovery is likely still several years away.

### 1100. Effects of 4-Day School Weeks on Older Adolescents: Examining Impacts on Academic Achievement, Attendance, and Behavior in High School


This research examined how 4-day school week schedules affect high school students using Oklahoma district data. The study found that 4-day school weeks decrease per-pupil bullying incidents by approximately 39% and fighting incidents by roughly 31%, though no meaningful changes were detected in test scores or attendance rates.

### 1101. Achievement and Growth for English Learners

```yaml
url: "https://www.nwea.org/research/publication/achievement-and-growth-for-english-learners/"
type: report
source: "NWEA Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [english-learners, formative-assessment, k-12, longitudinal]
```

This working paper examines academic achievement and growth patterns for English Learners from kindergarten through 4th grade across three distinct groups. All three EL groups had lower test scores than never-ELs throughout K-4, with notably different patterns in math versus reading development. The research indicates that summer academic support is essential, as English Learners experience greater skill loss during break periods compared to non-English learners.

---

---

### 1102. Within-Year Achievement Gains for English Learners

```yaml
url: "https://www.nwea.org/research/publication/within-year-achievement-gains-for-english-learners/"
type: report
source: "NWEA Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [english-learners, formative-assessment, k-12, longitudinal]
```

This working paper examines fall-to-spring achievement gains across grades K–8 for three groups of English Learners, analyzing data from 186,139 ever-ELs and their never-EL peers in 1,520 schools. The study reveals that ever-ELs and current-ELs make larger gains in the early grades but smaller gains in the middle grades, with findings indicating that better academic support is urgently needed for all multilingual students.

---

---

### 1103. Dual Language Education and Academic Growth

This 2020 working paper examines math and reading academic achievement and growth in grades 2 to 8 for Hispanic participants and nonparticipants of a Spanish-English dual language program. While dual language students demonstrated faster math growth in early grades, they did not learn more than their peers in the long run. The research reveals that programs aimed at addressing achievement gaps need to consider summer as well as school-year learning.

### 1107. Looking Back: How Prior-Year Attendance Impacts Starting Achievement

The study finds that students with moderate to chronic absenteeism start the following year significantly behind their peers academically, underscoring the compounding nature of attendance problems.

### 1108. Do High Flyers Maintain Their Altitude? Performance Trends of Top Students

```yaml

```

This 2013 study from the Thomas B. Fordham Institute tracked students who scored at or above the 90th percentile to examine whether high-achieving students maintained their performance levels. Analyzing data from over 91,000 middle and high school students, researchers found that approximately 60% of initially high-achieving students remained in that category, while those who dropped typically stayed at the 70th percentile or above.

### 1118. Test Score Patterns Across Three COVID-19-Impacted School Years

### 1131. Persistence and Convergence: The End-of-Kindergarten Outcomes of Pre-K Graduates and Their Nonattending Peers

```yaml
url: "https://www.nwea.org/research/publication/persistence-and-convergence-the-end-of-kindergarten-outcomes-of-pre-k-graduates-and-their-nonattending-peers/"
type: report
source: "NWEA Research"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, longitudinal, prekindergarten]
```

This study examined how pre-K benefits persist through kindergarten for 2,581 low-income children, finding that pre-K graduates outperformed nonattenders in achievement and executive functioning skills at the end of kindergarten, though initial advantages diminished by approximately half during the kindergarten year. The convergence between groups was attributed to nonattenders making greater gains in kindergarten rather than differences in classroom experiences.

### 1132. Leveled Literacy Intervention for Secondary Students: Results from a Randomized Controlled Trial in Oakland Schools

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/leveled-literacy-intervention-for-secondary-students-results-from-a-randomized-controlled-trial"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, professional-development, rct]
```

This RCT — the nation's first randomized controlled trial of Leveled Literacy Intervention (LLI) in secondary grades — examined implementation and outcomes across Oakland Unified School District secondary schools, where roughly half of students read multiple grade levels below level. Students did not receive the recommended minimum number of LLI sessions due to delayed starts, scheduling conflicts, and varied fidelity. The intervention showed no positive impact on reading comprehension and produced negative outcomes on ELA/literacy standards mastery, with students pulled from core classes particularly affected.

---

---

### 1133. Effectiveness of Reading and Mathematics Software Products: Findings from Two Student Cohorts

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/effectiveness-of-reading-and-mathematics-software-products-findings-from-two-student-cohorts"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [computer-assisted-learning, digital-learning-platforms, k-12, literacy, math-education, rct]
```

This congressionally mandated study evaluated ten computer-based reading and math software products across two student cohorts (2004–05 and 2005–06 school years), using an experimental design. Teacher experience with the software showed no systematic relationship to changes in outcomes between years. Only one product had statistically significant positive effects overall, raising questions about the educational value of educational software as deployed in these conditions.

---

---

### 1134. Achievement Effects of Four Early Elementary School Math Curricula: Findings from First Graders in 39 Schools

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/achievement-effects-of-four-early-elementary-school-math-curricula-findings-from-first-graders-in-39-schools"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, k-12, math-education, math-strategies, rct]
```

The largest experimental study of its kind, this federal report compared the effectiveness of four early math curricula (Math Expressions, Saxon Math, Investigations in Number/Data/Space, and Scott Foresman-Addison Wesley Mathematics) across 39 schools. Math Expressions and Saxon Math produced significantly higher first-grade achievement than the other two programs, with benefits particularly pronounced for students in low-performing, high-poverty schools.

---

---

### 1135. Menu of Measures: High-Quality Measures of Middle Years Math Student Outcomes

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/menu-of-measures-high-quality-measures-of-middle-years-math-student-outcomes"

license: null

```

Developed with a research panel for Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation grantees, this curated guide provides educators, district leaders, researchers, and funders with high-quality ways to measure middle-grades students' math skills, perceptions and beliefs, and math use beyond the classroom. It includes validity and reliability criteria and context-specific selection guidance, with a focus on improving math outcomes for Black, Latino, and economically disadvantaged middle-grade students.

---

### 1136. Measuring Children's Progress from Preschool Through Third Grade


This paper examines how to assess child outcomes when evaluating preschool program effectiveness, arguing that traditional standardized tests alone are insufficient for young children. The author advocates for observational measures spanning preschool through elementary grades and a multimethod assessment strategy that avoids high-stakes applications, highlighting the need for classroom quality measurement in program evaluations.

### 1137. Testing New Approaches to Math Tutoring: Lessons from Eight Evaluations

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/testing-new-approaches-to-math-tutoring-lessons-from-eight-evaluations"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, math-education, math-motivation, personalized-learning, rct, student-belonging]
```

This brief synthesizes findings from evaluations of eight tutoring programs piloting alternative approaches — including virtual and group tutoring — for students in grades 4–10 during the 2021–22 school year. Programs with high attendance, adequate staffing, and strong student-tutor relationships produced the best learning outcomes. The brief finds that group and virtual tutoring can boost math knowledge and may improve students' math confidence and sense of belonging.

### 1138. Examining Student and Teacher Math Learning: Lessons from Three Summer Programs

This brief evaluates three Gates Foundation-funded summer programs for grades 6–8 that used research-based curricula and instructional practices to support diverse learners during 2019–20. Students showed substantial grade improvements in the following semester, and teachers successfully incorporated new instructional approaches from training into their regular practice. The brief identifies actionable lessons around teacher training, program monitoring, and student recruitment.

### 1139. Math Corps Successfully Replicated

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/math-corps-successfully-replicated"

url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false

```

Mathematica independently evaluated the replication of Math Corps — a summer camp for middle school students that originated at Wayne State University — across three new cities (Cleveland, Philadelphia, and Utica) funded by the National Science Foundation (2017–19). All three replication sites successfully served their priority communities, maintained strong attendance, and produced statistically significant improvements on Math Corps assessments from camp start to completion.

### 1140. Sustaining Effective Teacher Practice: The Impact of the EL Education Language Arts Curriculum and Professional Development on Teachers' Instruction

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/sustaining-effective-teacher-practice-the-impact-of-the-el-education-language-arts-curriculum"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, professional-development, writing-instruction]
```

This i3-funded study examined how the Teacher Potential Project (TPP) — combining a Common Core-aligned EL Education Language Arts curriculum with intensive professional development — affected teachers' instructional practices compared to district-provided curricula. Significantly more TPP teachers encouraged higher-order thinking, had students cite textual evidence in writing, and engaged students in reading, writing, and speaking about texts, especially in the second year of implementation.

### 1141. Teacher Factors That Drive Improvement in Secondary Writing Outcomes: A Landscape Analysis

This Gates Foundation-funded brief synthesizes an academic literature review identifying instructional skills and practices that support writing improvements for grades 6–12 students. Teachers influence writing outcomes both through explicit instructional practices and through subtler means — how they engage with students, provide support, and offer feedback — making teacher development a critical lever for secondary writing improvement.

### 1142. What Matters for Student Achievement? Exploring Teacher Instructional Practices and the Role of School-Level and Student-Level Characteristics

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/what-matters-for-student-achievement-exploring-teacher-instructional-practices-and-the-role-of"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, longitudinal, professional-development]
```

This exploratory study analyzed data from 10,716 students taught by 214 ELA teachers across 63 schools to identify which instructional practices relate to higher student achievement. General instruction and classroom management practices — fostering engagement, facilitating discussion, minimizing disruptions, and maintaining a positive climate — showed significant associations with achievement, more so than specific reading/writing strategies. School characteristics like racial diversity, student-teacher ratios, and enrollment moderated these relationships.

This publication examines how KIPP, the nation's largest charter school network, developed a structured internal program to cultivate school leaders by turning the assistant principal experience into a deliberate leadership development pathway. Rather than relying solely on external recruitment, KIPP's strategies address the ongoing need for qualified principals through on-the-job training and competency-building for assistant principals.

### 1144. Implementation and Impacts of Pay-for-Performance: The 2010 Teacher Incentive Fund (TIF) Grantees After Three Years

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/teacher-incentive-fund-three-years-snapshot"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, professional-development, rct]
```

This NCEE study snapshot examines how districts implemented performance-based compensation systems funded through 2010 federal Teacher Incentive Fund grants. After three years, pay-for-performance had small positive impacts on students' math and reading achievement in the ten evaluated districts. However, few districts structured bonuses to align well with TIF guidance, and many teachers misunderstood their eligibility or potential earnings.

### 1145. Districts and Data: Developing Capacity for Effective Data Use

```yaml
license: null
tags: [formative-assessment, k-12, professional-development]
```

This issue brief examines the challenges school districts face in using data strategically, noting that while large interconnected datasets have raised educator awareness of data's value, many districts struggle to identify the right questions, datasets, or interpretive methods. The brief offers four recommendations: build a culture of data use, ask the right questions, correctly interpret findings, and dedicate adequate resources to analysis.

### 1146. How Community Colleges Address Basic Needs and Financial Stability of Low-Income Students to Boost College Completion

```yaml

description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [college-access, dropout-prevention, higher-ed, student-belonging]
```

This report examines the Working Students Success Network (WSSN), a comprehensive support strategy for low-income students and students of color across 19 community colleges, integrating financial literacy, budgeting, and career planning with credential attainment. The research found that receiving intensive one-on-one service mattered more than multi-pillar group services, though colleges could reach more students through group-based delivery when individualized approaches were adapted to individual needs.

### 1147. From Outreach to Enrollment: Strategies to Engage Adults in Education Beyond High School

```yaml
tags: [career-readiness, college-access, higher-ed]
```

This Lumina Foundation-funded brief examines marketing, outreach, and enrollment strategies employed by states receiving Adult Promise grants to engage adult learners (ages 25–64) in higher education. Key findings emphasize that appropriate messaging is critical because adult learners' priorities differ from traditional enrollees, high-touch personalized guidance through enrollment and financial aid is essential, and digital tools can make the enrollment process more convenient for adults balancing work and family demands.

This issue brief examines the integration of academic content — particularly mathematics — into career and technical education (CTE) curricula, based on feedback from CTE educators. The research finds that incorporating academic learning into CTE requires substantial time, effort, and resources, and highlights the real implementation challenges educators and curriculum developers must navigate when blending vocational training with academic content.

### 1149. The NIH Undergraduate Scholarship Program: Career Outcomes of Scholars and Non-Awarded Finalists

```yaml
license: null
tags: [career-readiness, higher-ed, longitudinal]
```

This evaluation compared career outcomes between NIH Undergraduate Scholarship Program recipients and finalist applicants who did not receive awards, examining whether the program effectively supports disadvantaged undergraduate science students in pursuing biomedical research careers. Scholars were achieving career milestones at high rates and making steady progress toward becoming biomedical researchers, substantially outpacing non-awarded finalists, suggesting the program is operating as intended.

### 1150. Disproportionality in School Discipline: An Assessment in Maryland through 2018

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/disproportionality-in-school-discipline-an-assessment-in-maryland-through-2018-study-snapshot"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, school-discipline]
```

This Regional Educational Laboratory Mid-Atlantic study analyzed disciplinary removal trends in Maryland schools from 2009/10 to 2017/18 using administrative data. Although overall exclusionary discipline rates declined, Black students and students with disabilities continued to be suspended and expelled at more than twice the rates of other students. Even for identical infractions, these groups were significantly more likely to receive out-of-school suspensions, suggesting implicit bias as a contributing factor.

---

---

### 1151. Preparing for Life After High School: The Characteristics and Experiences of Youth in Special Education

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/preparing-for-life-after-high-school-the-characteristics-and-experiences-of-youth-in-special"

description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [career-readiness, k-12, longitudinal]
```

This NCEE evaluation brief summarizes findings from the National Longitudinal Transition Study 2012, which surveyed secondary school youth with disabilities and their parents to assess IDEA implementation. Youth with disabilities were found to be more socioeconomically disadvantaged, to demonstrate lower academic performance, and to be less likely to take steps toward postsecondary education and employment, with youth with intellectual disabilities, autism, and multiple disabilities facing the highest risks.

### 1152. Building Teacher Capacity to Support English Language Learners in Schools Receiving School Improvement Grants

```yaml

description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [english-learners, k-12, professional-development, qualitative-research]
```

This NCEE evaluation brief examined staffing and professional development strategies for serving English Language Learners in 11 high-ELL schools receiving federal School Improvement Grant funding (2010–13). Few schools leveraged staffing strategies or hired with ELL expertise in mind, while teachers widely participated in ELL-related PD but received limited hours. Schools that devoted a greater share of PD to ELL topics had teachers more likely to report improved effectiveness.

### 1153. Not Just Numeracy and Literacy: Theory of Mind Development and School Readiness among Low-Income Children

```yaml

url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, longitudinal, prekindergarten, sel]
```

This longitudinal study of 120 low-income preschool and kindergarten children examined how theory of mind development contributes to school readiness, exploring links among theory of mind, understanding of teaching, learning behaviors, and literacy and numeracy skills at school entry. Children's understanding of teaching was particularly important — it predicted changes in literacy scores over time — underscoring that sociocognitive development matters for school readiness beyond traditional academic skills.

### 1154. Student Characteristics and Achievement in 22 KIPP Middle Schools

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/student-characteristics-and-achievement-in-22-kipp-middle-schools"

source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true

license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, longitudinal, math-education]
```

This multi-year evaluation of the Knowledge Is Power Program (KIPP) employed a matched comparison group design to examine impacts on disadvantaged students across 22 KIPP middle schools. For the vast majority of KIPP schools in the evaluation, impacts on state assessment scores in math and reading were positive, statistically significant, and educationally substantial, providing rigorous evidence for the model's effectiveness.

### 1155. Pre-Kindergarten Impacts Over Time: An Analysis of KIPP Charter Schools

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/pre-kindergarten-impacts-over-time-an-analysis-of-kipp-charter-schools"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [early-childhood, k-12, longitudinal, prekindergarten, rct]
```

This study tracked outcomes over five years to determine whether benefits from KIPP Pre-K enrollment persisted as students progressed through elementary school. After five years, KIPP Pre-K combined with KIPP early elementary school showed positive and statistically significant impacts on reading and math achievement, with potential advantages for executive function development. Reading skill improvements were sustained over time, though reading comprehension impacts largely dissipated by grade 2.

### 1156. Scaling Programs with Research Evidence and Effectiveness (SPREE)

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/scaling-programs-with-research-evidence-and-effectiveness-spree"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, longitudinal, meta-analysis]
```

Published in The Foundation Review, this article describes the SPREE process — Scaling Programs with Research Evidence and Effectiveness — a framework used by the Corporation for National and Community Service since 2016 to help foundations and grantees identify and scale effective interventions. SPREE uses evaluation and implementation science to determine which programs produce desired outcomes and which organizations are ready for expansion, and can help organizations develop evaluation cultures even when none previously existed.

---

---

### 1157. Challenges in Adolescent Reading Intervention: Evidence from a Randomized Control Trial

```yaml
url: "https://www.mathematica.org/publications/challenges-in-adolescent-reading-intervention-evidence-from-a-randomized-control-trial"
type: report
source: "Mathematica"
url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
date_added: 2026-05-10
doi: null
license: null
tags: [k-12, literacy, rct, response-to-intervention]
```

Reporting on the nation's first RCT of Leveled Literacy Intervention (LLI) in secondary grades in Oakland schools, this study found no measurable improvement in reading comprehension and adverse impacts on ELA/literacy standards mastery. Students pulled from regular classes for LLI were particularly negatively affected, possibly due to missing grade-level content. The research underscores the difficulty of adapting elementary interventions for adolescents without accounting for curriculum continuity.

---

---

This case study analysis examined how five Promise Neighborhoods grantees — part of a $100 million federal initiative across 48 lead agencies in 23 states — designed their programs, selected services, and navigated early implementation challenges. Promise Neighborhoods aim to build integrated support systems from early childhood through career preparation in high-poverty communities, offsetting the effects of poverty on children's educational and life outcomes.

### 1159. Missouri's Teacher Career Ladder Program

```yaml

url_confirmed: true
description_inferred: false
```
